## PAGE 00438: (2026x) Tabular view columns

- page_id: `254423012`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/254423012/2026x+Tabular+view+columns
- version_number: 4
- version_date: `2026-01-15T18:33:56.202+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) Model composition > (2026x) Views > (2026x) Creating views > (2026x) Creating tabular views
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Tabular views contain columns used to represent specific information about the exposed elements. See the information on this page to learn about table columns before you start [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Modeling tabular view columns' space=''].

##### Columns in tabular view definitions and usages

Tabular views and their definitions can differ in the table columns they contain:

- Tabular view definitions:
  - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Tabular views' space=''] come with predefined columns.
  - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Custom tabular view definitions' space=''] contain only the columns you specify.
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Creating tabular views' space=''] inherit columns from the view definitions they are defined by. You can also specify custom columns for them.

##### Column kinds

Currently, there are 2 model-based table column kinds available for modeling. The columns you create for a tabular view usage or definition must be defined by one of these column definitions, depending on the column's purpose. The table below lists:

- Column kinds and their descriptions.
- Relevant information regarding cell type according to column kind.
  - The cell type enables correct data representation and correct cell value type (e.g., String, Boolean, etc.) suggestions upon cell editing.
- Predefined attributes applicable to each column kind.

| Column | Description | Cells | Column attributes |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| ColumnByFeatureView | Dedicated to simple metadata feature value retrieval. It requires the expression to return only 1 metadata feature, like Element::declaredName or FeatureValue::value. The expression is specified for the feature columnFeature.>]]> | For ColumnByFeatureView, the cell type is calculated out of the metadata feature and does not need to be specified explicitly.The String, Element, Enum, Integer, Boolean, and Real cell values can be edited in the table view's diagrammatic representation for ColumnByFeatureView column cells. >]]> | isMultiline. It allows you to specify whether single-valued string cells should be displayed in a multiline format. The default value is false.>]]> |
| ColumnByExpressionView | Dedicated to complex metadata feature value retrieval. For example, when the value of the owned attribute needs to be retrieved, the feature chain expression is needed to access this information.>]]> | For ColumnByExpressionView, the cell type is specified by defining the cell rendering with the appropriate rendering definition. It works as a kind of filter for the type of wanted expression results.>]]>The editing of ColumnByExpressionView cells is not yet supported in the table view's diagrammatic representation.Rendering definitionsrendering def BooleanCellRendering :> TableCellRendering;rendering def ElementCellRendering :> TableCellRendering;rendering def EnumCellRendering :> TableCellRendering;rendering def IntegerCellRendering :> TableCellRendering; rendering def MetaobjectCellRendering :> TableCellRendering;rendering def RealCellRendering :> TableCellRendering;rendering def StringCellRendering :> TableCellRendering;rendering def TableCellRendering { calc getValue { in rowElement [0..1] : Metaobjects::Metaobject; } attribute isMultiValued [0..1] : ScalarValues::Boolean; } | isMultiline. It allows you to specify whether single-valued string cells should be displayed in a multiline format.The default value is false. >]]> |

## PAGE 00439: (2026x) Tabular views

- page_id: `254422149`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/254422149/2026x+Tabular+views
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2025-12-03T08:36:45.843+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) Model management > (2026x) Working with views > (2026x) View kinds
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Tabular views (i.e., tables) allow you to represent and modify information about the model elements and/or relationships between them.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Tabular views

Specific tabular views you create in the modeling tool are tabular view usages defined by appropriate standard predefined tabular view definitions.

- For tabular view creation procedures, see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Creating tabular views' space=''] page.
- For tabular view columns modeling procedures, see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Modeling tabular view columns' space=''] page.
- Additionally, you can create [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Custom tabular view definitions' space=''] .

There are multiple predefined tabular view definitions available in the modeling tool, each designed for specific purposes:

| View definitions | Description | Columns | Availability |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Standard predefined tabular view definitions |  |  |  |
| Generic Table | Standard predefined multi-purpose generic tabular view for element representation and modeling. | Declared NameDeclared Short NameDocumentationQualified NameOwnerOwned Element | Via Textual EditorVia the Create View command dialog/submenu |
| Requirements Table | Standard predefined tabular view for requirements representation and modeling.It subclassifies the NonStandardLibraryElementFilter view definition, which includes multiple filters that exclude various standard library elements that are unlikely to be relevant for display in views. | Declared NameReq IDDocumentation |  |

##### Tabular view areas

Views are displayed in the View Editor tab.

- Table toolbar allows you to quickly access the options for managing data in the table. See the page.
- Grid displays data according to the exposed elements and their filters. For information on element display in tables, see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Displaying elements in tabular views' space=''] page. The grid consists of:
  - Rows that appear along the side of the table, and are labeled using numbers starting with 1. Each table row represents an element .
  - Columns that appear along the top of the table, and are labeled according to the specified metadata feature or named by hand, i.e., by [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Modeling tabular view columns' space='']. Each table column represents specific element data.
    - You can also modify tabular view columns. See the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Modeling tabular view columns' space=''] page.
    - Column cells can be .
  - Cells are the intersections of every row and column, which represent element data values.
- Table info area displays table information.

**[IMAGE alt='' src='']**

###### Tabular view areas presented in a standard predefined tabular 'Generic Table' view.

###### Tabular view toolbar buttons

The table below shows the buttons available in the tabular view toolbar.

| Button | Shortcut keys | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Refresh | - | Click the button to update the displayed table content. |
| Open in Textual Editor | - | Click the button to open the table's textual notation in the Textual Editor. |

##### Filtering and/or sorting tabular view column cells

#### WARNING: Tabular view loading

Tabular view loading

Tabular views load only the content that is immediately visible; additional rows and cell data are loaded as you scroll.

- If you filter and/or sort a partially loaded column cells, a notification icon [ATTACHMENT filename='filtering or sorting partially loaded cells notification.png'] appears in the column header. Scroll through the entire table to load all content; once fully loaded, retry to filter/sort. [ATTACHMENT filename='filtering or sorting partially loaded cells.png']

**NOTE**: filtering and sorting do not persist and reset automatically once you reopen the tabular view.

###### Filtering tabular view column cells

To filter tabular view column cells

1. In the tabular view, right-click the header of the column whose cells you want to filter and click Filter Column in the shortcut menu.
2. Do one of the following:
  1. Expand the Filter by Values section. Do one of the following:
    1. In the Values column, select the check boxes for the values you want to display in the column.
    2. Type the value name in the input field to narrow the search, select the Apply filter on refined values check box, and select the check boxes for the values you want to display in the column.
  2. Expand the Filter by Conditions section.
    1. Select the condition from the dropdown menu and type the value you are looking for in the input field.
3. Click OK .
4. (Optional) To reset the filter, right-click the column header with the applied filter. In the shortcut menu, do one of the following:
  1. Click Reset Column Filter .
  2. Click Filter Column , then Reset . Click OK .

###### Sorting tabular view cells

To sort tabular view cells

- In the tabular view, click the header of the column whose cells you want to sort. The Sorting icon is displayed in the header, indicating the current sorting order. Click the column header to change the sorting order. Sorting order types[IMAGE alt='' src=''] indicates that cells in the column are sorted alphabetically in ascending order. This is the default mode.[IMAGE alt='' src=''] indicates that cells in the column are sorted alphabetically in descending order.

Name</span></li><li data-uuid="f28308b9-ea91-4a73-9c9a-cc76259ae575"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Declared Short Name</span></li><li data-uuid="b5ec87b6-6a43-41e5-b717-11d9828504be"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Documentation</span></li><li data-uuid="23651ec8-98a9-47ab-a62f-6c5045429f57"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Qualified Name</span></li><li data-uuid="3ac82e45-954d-4a30-8d68-b1453a81ab30"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Owner</span></li><li data-uuid="34fc12e5-c9f9-4211-aedf-194a771383b7"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Owned Element</span></li></ul></td><td style="width: 4.54066%;" rowspan="2"><ul style="text-align: left;"><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Via Textual Editor</span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Via the Create View command dialog/submenu</span></li></ul></td></tr><tr><td style="width: 8.99482%;"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Requirements Table</span></td><td style="width: 38.6172%;"><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Standard predefined tabular view for requirements representation and modeling.</span></p><p>It subclassifies the <em>NonStandardLibraryElementFilter</em> view definition, which includes multiple filters that exclude various standard library elements that are unlikely to be relevant for display in views.</p></td><td><ul><li data-uuid="9c5e867b-ebca-4853-b342-48e4dcf17b1e"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Declared Name</span></li><li data-uuid="84dc0f64-ced7-4dcb-9841-b7cecc5b358e"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Req ID</span></li><li data-uuid="5f6fad88-c000-4c08-9bfe-23a55dda5ff2"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Documentation</span></li></ul></td></tr></tbody></table><h3>Tabular view areas</h3><p>Views are displayed in the View Editor tab.</p><ul><li><strong><span class="confluence-link">Table toolbar </span></strong>allows you to quickly access the options for managing data in the table. See the <ac:link ac:anchor="Tabular view toolbar buttons" /> page.</li><li><strong>Grid</strong><span> </span>displays data according to the exposed elements and their filters. For information on element display in tables, see the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Displaying elements in tabular views" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Displaying elements in tabular views]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> page.<br />The grid consists of:<br /><ul><li><strong>Rows </strong>that appear along the side of the table, and are labeled using numbers starting with 1. Each table<span> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">row represents an element</span><em>.<br /></em></li><li><strong><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="53608ec1-9cc2-4bc6-a081-14bc9fc7047b">Columns</ac:inline-comment-marker> </strong>that appear along the top of the table, and are <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="c9f9ea1c-3b09-47fa-b5e3-18056e064aeb">labeled according to the specified metadata feature or named by hand, i.e., by <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Modeling tabular view columns" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[naming the column view usage in the asTable renderer]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</ac:inline-comment-marker> Each table column<span> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">represents specific element data.</span><em><br /></em><ul><li data-uuid="1f28ea9b-b26b-4396-abd1-b01655db02d7">You can also modify tabular view columns. See the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Modeling tabular view columns" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Modeling tabular view columns]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> page.</li><li data-uuid="aaed012b-b4c9-4c53-aeb0-92759f8fd2d3">Column cells can be <ac:link ac:anchor="Filtering and/or sorting tabular view column cells"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[sorted and filtered]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>. </li></ul></li><li><strong>Cells </strong>are the intersections of every row and column, which represent element data values.</li></ul></li><li><strong>Table info area </strong>displays table information. </li></ul><p><br /></p><p style="text-align: center;"><span style="color:var(--ds-background-accent-red-bolder,#c9372c);"><strong><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="table_areas.png" /></ac:image></strong></span></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Tabular view areas presented in a standard predefined tabular 'Generic Table' view.</h6><h4>Tabular view toolbar buttons</h4><p>The table below shows the buttons available in the tabular view toolbar.</p><table class="relative-table wrapped" style="width: 90.189%;"><colgroup><col style="width: 21.4862%;" /><col style="width: 12.4882%;" /><col style="width: 65.9856%;" /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th style="text-align: left;">Button</th><th style="text-align: left;">Shortcut keys</th><th style="text-align: left;">Description</th></tr><tr><td><div class="content-wrapper"><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="refresh_table_button.png" /></ac:image>Refresh</p></div></td><td><span>-</span></td><td><div class="content-wrapper"><p>Click the button to update the displayed table content.</p></div></td></tr><tr><td><div class="content-wrapper"><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="open_in_textual_editor_button.png" /></ac:image>Open in Textual Editor</p></div></td><td><span>-</span></td><td><div class="content-wrapper"><p>Click the button to open the table's textual notation in the Textual Editor.</p></div></td></tr></tbody></table><h3 class="auto-cursor-target"><span>Filtering and/or sorting tabular view column cells</span></h3><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="72ed2037-cbfc-48ec-be3f-9ec8c2d52b6a"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Tabular view loading</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Tabular views load only the content that is immediately visible; additional rows and cell data are loaded as you scroll.</p><ul><li data-uuid="fa3fcd37-c950-4c26-8ddc-6ee5794b357a">If you filter and/or sort a partially loaded column cells, a notification icon <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="filtering or sorting partially loaded cells notification.png" /></ac:image> appears in the column header. Scroll through the entire table to load all content; once fully loaded, retry to filter/sort.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="filtering or sorting partially loaded cells.png" /></ac:image></li></ul><p><strong>NOTE</strong>: filtering and sorting do not persist and reset automatically once you reopen the tabular view.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h4 class="auto-cursor-target"><span>Filtering tabular view column cells</span></h4><p>To filter <span>tabular view column cells</span></p><hr /><ol><li data-uuid="09f8b307-58eb-4189-86a9-982c83c392f5">In the tabular view, right-click the header of the<strong> </strong>column whose cells you want to filter and click <strong>Filter Column</strong> in the shortcut menu.</li><li data-uuid="013bec58-2b3a-4465-9d98-6e58b3dff05c">Do one of the following:<ol><li>Expand the <strong>Filter by Values</strong> section. Do one of the following:<ol><li>In the <strong>Values</strong> column, select the check boxes for the values you want to display in the column.</li><li>Type the value name in the input field to narrow the search, select the <strong>Apply filter on refined values</strong> check box, and select the check boxes for the values you want to display in the column.</li></ol></li><li>Expand the <strong>Filter by Conditions</strong> section.<ol><li>Select the condition from the dropdown menu and type the value you are looking for in the input field. </li></ol></li></ol></li><li data-uuid="a4e021bc-1431-4a95-ba93-e889c1b1c043">Click <strong>OK</strong>.</li><li data-uuid="8e811aad-39ab-4979-bfc8-98abc76d1490">(Optional) To reset the filter, right-click the column header with the applied filter. In the shortcut menu, do one of the following:<ol><li>Click <strong>Reset Column Filter</strong>.</li><li>Click <strong>Filter Column</strong>, then <strong>Reset</strong>. Click <strong>OK</strong>.</li></ol></li></ol><h4>Sorting tabular view cells </h4><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">To sort tabular view cells</span></p><hr /><ul><li data-uuid="a0641c12-3bb2-4ee2-860e-bed49fa1c271">In the tabular view, click the header of the column whose cells you want to sort. The <strong>Sorting</strong> icon is displayed in the header, indicating the current sorting order. Click the column header to change the sorting order.<br /><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0f1484ef-48ac-48c0-a06a-a94104db72ba"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Sorting order types</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="sorting_1.png" /></ac:image> indicates that cells in the column are sorted alphabetically in ascending order. This is the default mode.</span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="sorting_2.png" /></ac:image> indicates that cells in the column are sorted alphabetically in descending order.</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li></ul>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=254422511 space=SYSML2P version=2 -->
## PAGE 00440: (2026x) Terminate action

- page_id: `254422511`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/254422511/2026x+Terminate+action
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-10-14T15:22:15.591+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) Model composition > (2026x) Behavior > (2026x) Actions
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A terminate action usage is used to terminate the performance of some other action. The terminated action ends its performance by the completion of the terminate action usage. The terminated action may be given as an argument to the terminate action usage. If not given explicitly, by default, a terminate action usage terminates its own immediately containing action.

##### Specifying terminate actions

###### Specifying terminate actions via the Textual Editor

To specify terminate actions via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, place the cursor where you want to create the terminate action and do one of the following:
  1. To terminate the immediately containing action:
    1. For a named terminate action usage, declare the keyword action , specify the element name, and then the keyword terminate .
    2. For an unnamed terminate action usage, declare the keyword terminate .
  2. To terminate a non-immediately containing action:
    1. For a named terminate action usage, declare the keyword action , specify the element name, then the keyword terminate , followed by the name of the action you want to terminate.
    2. For an unnamed terminate action usage, declare the keyword terminate, followed by the name of the action you want to terminate.
2. Click the Synchronize button.

// ------ terminating the immediately containing action:

actionrun{ 
firststart; 
actionenable; 
thenactionproceed; 
thenterminate;// unnamed terminate action that terminates 'run' action 
} 
actionrunAction{ 
actionrun{ 
firststart; 
actionenable; 
thenactionproceed; 
thenactionterminateRunterminate;// named terminate action 'terminateRun' that terminates 'run' 
} 
}

// ------ terminating a non-immediately containing action:

actionrunAction{ 
actionrun{ 
firststart; 
actionenable; 
thenactionproceed; 
thenterminaterunAction;// unnamed terminate action that terminates 'runAction' 
} 
} 
actionrunAction{ 
actionrun{ 
firststart; 
actionenable; 
thenactionproceed; 
thenactionterminateRunActionterminaterunAction;// named terminate action 'terminateRunAction' that terminates 'runAction' 
} 
}

###### Specifying terminate actions via the view palette

To specify terminate actions via the view palette

1. In the view palette, under the Actions group, click the **terminate** button to create a terminate action usage element.
2. Click on an action symbol in the view pane where you want to create the terminate symbol.

style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">        </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">then</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">action</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">terminateRunAction</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">terminate</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">runAction</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">;  </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-green-bolder,#164b35);">// named terminate action 'terminateRunAction' that terminates 'runAction'</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">    }</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">}</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h4 style="text-align: left;">Specifying terminate actions via the view palette</h4><p style="text-align: left;">To specify terminate actions via the view palette</p><hr style="text-align: left;" /><ol style="text-align: left;"><li data-uuid="20da99f3-d60e-46fe-96fc-5a70be0e7c3b">In the view palette, under the Actions group, click the<span> <strong>terminate </strong></span>button to create a terminate action usage element.</li><li data-uuid="a5d3c826-ca7c-43bd-980c-1e2a7afc8400">Click on an action symbol in the view pane where you want to create the terminate symbol.</li></ol>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=254422059 space=SYSML2P version=4 -->
## PAGE 00441: (2026x) Textual Editor features

- page_id: `254422059`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/254422059/2026x+Textual+Editor+features
- version_number: 4
- version_date: `2025-12-03T08:34:25.236+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) Model management > (2026x) Working with Textual Editor
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

See the sections on this page for the Textual Editor features that can ease your workflow. See also [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Command Palette' space=''] and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Find dialog' space=''] pages.

##### Autocompletion

The Textual Editor provides an autocompletion function.

- Invoke the autocompletion dialog by pressing Ctrl+Space shortcut keys. Use the up and down keyboard buttons to navigate the suggestions and click the selection.

Use the **Preferred Element Name for Autocompletion**[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Environment options' space=''] to specify which element name should be suggested in context-sensitive autocompletion.

Two types of autocompletion may be suggested in the autocompletion dialog:

- Snippets
  - Suggests keywords. Selecting the keyword creates the keyword with its notational structure.
  - Snippets are indicated with the square symbol. [ATTACHMENT filename='Autocompletion_1.png']
- Context-sensitive autocompletion
  - Suggests already-created elements' names and library packages.
  - Context-sensitive autocompletion is indicated with a box symbol. [ATTACHMENT filename='Autocompletion_2.png']

##### Folding the textual notation

Folding the element's body allows you to easily hide it in the textual notation if its visibility is not needed. It can be useful when the textual notation is extensive. The folding is only persistent while the Textual Editor is open; the element is unfolded upon the reopening of the Textual Editor.

To fold/unfold the textual notation

1. Locate the element whose body you want to fold.
2. Move the cursor to the left margin of the element keyword, next to the line number.
3. Do one of the following:
  1. Folding.
    1. Click the down-facing arrow to fold the body of an element. [ATTACHMENT filename='folding.png']
  2. Unfolding.
    1. Click the right-facing arrow to unfold it. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
    2. Click the three dots next to the opening braces of the folded body. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

You can also hold Shift and click the arrow. If the selected element contains elements with bodies, the latter are folded upon the first click of the arrow (this applies only to the first level of elements with bodies). The second click folds the entire body of the selected element. Unfolding works the same way: the first click unfolds the selected element, and the second unfolds the contained elements with folded bodies.

##### Textual Editor shortcut menu

Right-clicking anywhere in the Textual Editor pane opens the Textual Editor shortcut menu. The table below outlines the commands available in the shortcut menu.

| Command | Shortcut keys | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Go to Definition | Ctrl+F12 | Displays the definition of the selected keyword. |
| Go to Symbol... | Ctrl+Shift+O | Opens the Command Palette for element search by name. Type the element name you want to search for after the '@' symbol to navigate to the searched element in the textual notation. |
| Select in Containment Tree | Alt+B | Selects the element Containment tree. |
| Specification |  | Opens the Specification panel for the element. |
| Open View |  | Opens the view tab for the element. Available only for a view usage with a specified renderer. |
| Peek | Alt+F12 | Displays the Peek submenu:Peek Definition - displays the definition of the selected element. |
| Change All Occurrences | Ctrl+F2 | Selects all occurrences of the same selection. |
| Format Document | Shift+Alt+F | Formats the textual notation, such as alignment, spacing, etc. The command is disabled if the notation contains any errors. |
| Cut | Delete | Deletes the selection from the notation. |
| Copy | Ctrl+C | Copies the selection in the notation. |
| Paste | Ctrl+V | Pastes the copied selection in the notation. |
| Command Palette | F1 | Opens the Command Palette containing various Textual Editor navigation and editing commands. |

##### Textual Editor shortcut keys

The table below lists additional shortcut keys that can be used in the Textual Editor in addition to the shortcut keys indicated for the commands in the Textual Editor toolbar, the shortcut menu, and the Command Palette.

| Shortcut key | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Shift+Right | Selects characters one by one from the cursor to the right. |
| Ctrl+Shift+Right | Selects words one by one from the cursor to the right. |
| Ctrl+Shift+Left | Selects words one by one from the cursor to the left. |
| Alt+Up | Moves the line of text where the cursor is positioned up a line. |
| Alt+Down | Moves the line of text where the cursor is positioned down a line. |
| Shift+Alt+UpShift+Alt+Down | Copies the line of text where the cursor is positioned above or below the copied line. |
| Ctrl+Left | Jumps the cursor to the next word on the left. |
| Ctrl+Right | Jumps the cursor to the next word on the right. |
| Ctrl+F | Invokes the Find dialog. |
| Ctrl+Space | Invokes the autocompletion dialog. |

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=254422195 space=SYSML2P version=3 -->
## PAGE 00442: (2026x) Textual notation import/export

- page_id: `254422195`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/254422195/2026x+Textual+notation+import+export
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2025-11-10T17:59:07.779+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) Model management > (2026x) Working with Textual Editor > (2026x) Managing textual notation
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can export SysML v2 project namespaces into *.sysml* textual notation files which you can later import into your projects.

The **Smart Generation Timeout** environment option allows you to specify the timeout duration for smart generation in seconds. During textual notation generation, the tool generates the shortest qualified names. If the timeout is specified, a simpler name is generated once the time limit is reached. Learn more on the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Environment options' space=''] page.

##### Exporting textual notation

To export textual notation

1. Do one of the following:
  1. Export via File Menu:
    1. In the main menu, click File > Export To > SysML v2 Textual Notation . The Select namespaces window opens.
    2. In the Select namespaces window, in the All namespaces column, select the namespaces whose textual notation you want to export and click the Move [IMAGE alt='' src=''] button.
  2. Export via Containment tree:
    1. In the Containment tree, select the root namespace(s) whose textual notation you want to export.
    2. Right-click the selected namespace(s) and in the shortcut menu, click Tools > SysML v2 Textual Notation . The Select namespaces window opens.
2. Review the selected namespace(s) in the Selected namespaces column.
3. In the Select folder section, click the [ATTACHMENT filename='select_folder_button.png'] button, and in the Select Folder window, select the location where the exported textual notation file will be saved. Click Select .
4. (Optional) Select the Clean existing sysml files in output folder check box to delete all .sysml files in the output folder upon export.
5. Click Export . [ATTACHMENT filename='select_namespaces_window.png']

##### Importing textual notation

To import textual notation

1. In the main menu, click File > Import From > SysML v2 Textual Notation . The Select SysML v2 File window opens.
2. In the Select SysML v2 File window select the .sysml file you want to import and click Open . The selected file is imported into a separate root namespace.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>You can export SysML v2 project namespaces into <em>.sysml</em> textual notation files which you can later import into your projects.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="182bfe64-fdf3-42d3-bf47-1840ca677b0b"><ac:rich-text-body><p>The <strong>Smart Generation Timeout</strong> environment option allows you to <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">specify the timeout duration for smart generation in seconds. During textual notation generation, the tool generates the shortest qualified names. If the timeout is specified, a simpler name is generated once the time limit is reached. Learn more on the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Environment options" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Environment options]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> page.</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3 style="text-align: left;">Exporting textual notation</h3><p style="text-align: left;">To export textual notation</p><hr /><ol><li style="text-align: left;">Do one of the following:<ol><li>Export via File Menu:<ol><li>In the main menu, click <strong>File </strong>&gt; <strong>Export To</strong> &gt; <strong>SysML v2 Textual Notation</strong>. The <strong>Select namespaces</strong> window opens.</li><li>In the <strong>Select namespaces</strong> window, in the <strong>All namespaces</strong> column, select the namespaces whose textual notation you want to export and click the <strong>Move <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="move_button.png" /></ac:image> </strong>button. </li></ol></li><li>Export via Containment tree:<ol><li style="text-align: left;">In the Containment tree, select the root namespace(s) whose textual notation you want to export.</li><li style="text-align: left;">Right-click the selected namespace(s) and in the shortcut menu, click <strong>Tools </strong>&gt; <strong>SysML v2 Textual Notation</strong>. The <strong>Select namespaces</strong> window opens.</li></ol></li></ol></li><li>Review the selected namespace(s) in the <strong>Selected namespaces</strong> column.</li><li>In the <strong>Select folder</strong> section, click the <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="select_folder_button.png" /></ac:image><strong> </strong>button, and in the <strong>Select Folder</strong> window, select the location where the exported textual notation file will be saved. Click <strong>Select</strong>.</li><li>(Optional) Select the <strong>Clean existing sysml files in output folder</strong> check box to delete all <em>.sysml</em> files in the output folder upon export.</li><li>Click <strong>Export</strong>.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="select_namespaces_window.png" /></ac:image></li></ol><h3 style="text-align: left;">Importing textual notation</h3><p style="text-align: left;">To import textual notation</p><hr /><ol><li data-uuid="2386fb53-2caa-448d-b40c-0fe43db2bcaf">In the main menu, click <strong>File </strong>&gt; <strong>Import From</strong> &gt; <strong>SysML v2 Textual Notation</strong>. The <strong>Select SysML v2 File </strong>window opens.</li><li>In the <strong>Select SysML v2 File </strong>window select the <em>.sysml</em> file you want to import and click <strong>Open</strong>. The selected file is imported into a separate root namespace.</li></ol>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=314182526 space=SYSML2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 00443: (2026x) Timeslice

- page_id: `314182526`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/314182526/2026x+Timeslice
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-07-01T14:57:20.963+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) Model composition > (2026x) Common elements > (2026x) Occurrences
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A timeslice is a type of suboccurrence representing a portion of the occurrence's lifetime.

##### Specifying or creating elements as timeslices

###### Specifying or creating elements as timeslices via the Textual Editor

To specify or create elements as timeslices via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, declare the *timeslice* keyword prior to the declaration of the element that you want to specify as a timeslice.
2. Click the **Synchronize**button. timeslicepartcarInMotion;

###### Specifying elements as timeslices via the shortcut menu

To specify elements as timeslices via the shortcut menu

- Right-click a usage element in the Containment tree or its symbol in a view and in its shortcut menu, in the Properties group, click **Portion Kind**, and in its submenu, click **timeslice** .

###### Creating elements as timeslices via the view palette

To create elements as timeslices via the view palette

1. In the view palette, under the Items/Ports/Attributes group, do one of the following:
  1. To create an occurrence usage timeslice or one that will correspond to the element kind for which the timeslice will be created:
    1. Click the**timeslice** button.
    2. Click in the view pane or the element symbol for which you want the timeslice to be created:
      1. Creating an element on the empty space of the view pane creates an occurrence usage element as a timeslice by default.
      2. Creating an element on another element symbol creates a timeslice for it:
        1. If the element symbol is a definition, the created timeslice is also a usage (e.g. part) that corresponds to the definition element for which the timeslice is created (e.g. part def).
        2. If the element is a usage, the created timeslice is also a usage of the same kind as the element for which the timeslice is created.
  2. To specify the timeslice's element kind:
    1. Click the down-facing arrow next to the timeslice button, and in its dropdown menu, click any timeslice... and select the specific element kind you need.
    2. Click in the view pane or the element symbol for which you want to create the timeslice.

If a timeslice element is created for an element specified as an individual, the timeslice is also created as an individual by default.

element on another element symbol creates a timeslice for it:<ol><li>If the element symbol is a definition, the created timeslice is also a usage (e.g. part) that corresponds to the definition element for which the timeslice is created (e.g. part def). </li><li>If the element is a usage, the created timeslice is also a usage of the same kind as the element for which the timeslice is created. </li></ol></li></ol></li></ol></li><li>To specify the timeslice's element kind:<ol><li>Click the down-facing arrow next to the <strong>timeslice</strong> button, and in its dropdown menu, click <strong>any timeslice...</strong> and select the specific element kind you need. </li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Click in the view pane or the element symbol for which you want to create the timeslice.</span></li></ol></li></ol></li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f8c5b755-133f-4037-8605-679d82cc791b"><ac:rich-text-body><p>If a timeslice element is created for an element specified as an individual, the timeslice is also created as an individual by default.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=254421985 space=SYSML2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 00444: (2026x) Toolbars

- page_id: `254421985`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/254421985/2026x+Toolbars
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T14:33:03.826+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) Project management > (2026x) Modeling tool environment > (2026x) User interface
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### EXCERPT: Restoring default toolbars

INLINE

Toolbars help to speed up your work when performing commonly used tasks. Your modeling tool has two types of toolbars:

- Main toolbars - contain commands for basic project-related tasks and are located at the top of the modeling tool window, right under the main menu.
- View toolbars - contain commands for working with element symbols and are located at the top of the view pane.

To show or hide different toolbars

- In the main menu, click View , select either Main Toolbars , and in the displayed menu select/clear the check boxes of the toolbars that you want to show/hide.
- Right-click the desired toolbar area and in the shortcut menu select/clear the check boxes of the toolbars that you want to show/hide.

##### Customizing toolbars

You can customize existing toolbars by adding or removing buttons and also create new custom toolbars.

To add a new toolbar

1. Do one of the following:
  - In the main menu, select View > Main Toolbars > Customize .
  - Right-click the toolbar area, and in the shortcut menu select Customize .
2. In the Customize Toolbars dialog, select the desired toolbar group and click the Add > New Toolbar .
3. Enter the name of the new toolbar and click OK .

After completing the above steps, a new toolbar is created in the selected toolbar group, and you can choose to show/hide it in the shortcut menu of the respective toolbar area. Note that the newly created toolbar does not have any buttons, so you need to add them after creating it.

To add a new button to a toolbar

1. Do one of the following:
  - In the main menu, select View > Main Toolbars > Customize .
  - Right-click the toolbar area, and in the shortcut menu select Customize .
2. In the Customize Toolbars dialog, select the desired toolbar and click the Add > Button .
3. In the Add Button dialog, select the desired button or command and click OK .
4. Click OK one more time to close the Customize Toolbars dialog.

A new button is added to the selected toolbar. To add more than one button, repeat steps 2 and 3 as many times as you need.

To remove a toolbar or button

1. Do one of the following:
  - In the main menu, select View > Main Toolbars > Customize .
  - Right-click the toolbar area, and in the shortcut menu select Customize .
2. In the Customize Toolbars dialog, select the toolbar or button you want to remove and click the Remove button.

After completing the above steps, the selected toolbar or button is removed from the respective toolbar area or toolbar. Toolbars that have been removed do not appear in the menu for selecting which toolbars you want to show.

#### NOTE: Restoring default toolbars

Restoring default toolbars

To undo all the commands for customizing toolbars, open the **Customize Toolbars** dialog, as described in step 1 above, and click the **Reset to Defaults** button.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="excerpt" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c0cfe6be-5ec8-4144-b033-88ea8b060b92"><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Toolbars help to speed up your work when performing commonly used tasks. Your modeling tool has two types of toolbars:</p><ul><li>Main toolbars - contain commands for basic project-related tasks and are located at the top of the modeling tool window, right under the main menu.</li><li>View toolbars - contain commands for working with element symbols and are located at the top of the view pane.</li></ul><p><br /></p><p>To show or hide different toolbars</p><hr /><ul><li>In the main menu, click <strong>View</strong>, select either <strong>Main Toolbars</strong>, and in the displayed menu<strong> </strong>select/clear the check boxes of the toolbars that you want to show/hide.</li><li>Right-click the desired toolbar area and in the shortcut menu select/clear the check boxes of the toolbars that you want to show/hide.</li></ul><h3>Customizing toolbars</h3><p>You can customize existing toolbars by adding or removing buttons and also create new custom toolbars.</p><p><br /></p><p>To add a new toolbar</p><hr /><ol><li>Do one of the following:<ul><li>In the main menu, select <strong>View</strong> &gt; <strong>Main Toolbars</strong> &gt; <strong>Customize</strong>.</li><li>Right-click the toolbar area, and in the shortcut menu select <strong>Customize</strong>.</li></ul></li><li>In the <strong>Customize Toolbars</strong> dialog, select the desired toolbar group and click the <strong>Add</strong> &gt;<strong> New Toolbar</strong>.</li><li>Enter the name of the new toolbar and click <strong>OK</strong>. </li></ol><p><br />After completing the above steps, a new toolbar is created in the selected toolbar group, and you can choose to show/hide it in the shortcut menu of the respective toolbar area. Note that the newly created toolbar does not have any buttons, so you need to add them after creating it.</p><p><br /></p><p>To add a new button to a toolbar</p><hr /><ol><li>Do one of the following:<ul><li>In the main menu, select <strong>View</strong> &gt; <strong>Main Toolbars</strong> &gt; <strong>Customize</strong>.</li><li>Right-click the toolbar area, and in the shortcut menu select <strong>Customize</strong>.</li></ul></li><li>In the <strong>Customize Toolbars</strong> dialog, select the desired toolbar and click the <strong>Add</strong> &gt;<strong> Button</strong>. </li><li>In the <strong>Add Button</strong> dialog, select the desired button or command and click <strong>OK</strong>.</li><li>Click <strong>OK</strong> one more time to close the <strong>Customize Toolbars</strong> dialog.</li></ol><p><br />A new button is added to the selected toolbar. To add more than one button, repeat steps 2 and 3 as many times as you need.</p><p><br /></p><p>To remove a toolbar or button</p><hr /><ol><li>Do one of the following:<ul><li>In the main menu, select <strong>View</strong> &gt; <strong>Main Toolbars</strong> &gt; <strong>Customize</strong>.</li><li>Right-click the toolbar area, and in the shortcut menu select <strong>Customize</strong>.</li></ul></li><li>In the <strong>Customize Toolbars</strong> dialog, select the toolbar or button you want to remove and click the <strong>Remove</strong> button.</li></ol><p><br />After completing the above steps, the selected toolbar or button is removed from the respective toolbar area or toolbar. Toolbars that have been removed do not appear in the menu for selecting which toolbars you want to show.</p><p><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="67428948-3ddd-4460-9978-eac77bf76b73"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Restoring default toolbars</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>To undo all the commands for customizing toolbars, open the <strong>Customize Toolbars</strong> dialog, as described in step 1 above, and click the <strong>Reset to Defaults</strong> button.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=254422446 space=SYSML2P version=2 -->
## PAGE 00447: (2026x) Transfers between actions

- page_id: `254422446`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/254422446/2026x+Transfers+between+actions
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-12-03T08:59:05.176+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) Model composition > (2026x) Behavior > (2026x) Actions
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The case when there is a transfer of items between actions that may itself take time or have modeled properties can be modeled using the following options:

- A flow connection represents a flow in which the transfer can be ongoing while both the source and target action are being performed.
  - See the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Flow' space=''] page.
- A succession flow connection represents a flow that imposes the additional succession constraint that the transfer cannot begin until the source action completes and the target action cannot start until the transfer has completed.
  - See the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Succession flow' space=''] page.
- Transfers can also be performed using send and accept action usages . In this case, the source and target of the transfer do not have to be explicitly connected with a flow. Instead, the source of the transfer is specified using a send action usage contained in some source part or action, while the target is given by an accept action usage in some destination part or action. A send action usage includes an expression that is evaluated to provide the values to be transferred, and it specifies the destination to which those values are to be sent. An accept action usage specifies the type of values that can be received by the action. When a send action performed in the source is matched with a compatible accept action performed in the destination, then the transfer of values from the origin to the destination can be completed.
  - See the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Send action' space=''] page.
  - See the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Accept action' space=''] page.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>The case when there is a transfer of items between actions that may itself take time or have modeled properties can be modeled using the following options: </p><ul><li>A <strong>flow connection </strong>represents a flow in which the transfer can be ongoing while both the source and target action are being performed.<ul><li>See the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Flow" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Flow]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> page.</li></ul></li><li>A <strong>succession flow connection </strong>represents a flow that imposes the additional succession constraint that the transfer cannot begin until the source action completes and the target action cannot start until the transfer has completed.<br /><ul><li>See the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Succession flow" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Succession flow]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> page.</li></ul></li><li>Transfers can also be performed using <strong>send and accept action usages</strong>. In this case, the source and target of the transfer do not have to be explicitly connected with a flow. Instead, the source of the transfer is specified using a send action usage contained in some source part or action, while the target is given by an accept action usage in some destination part or action. A send action usage includes an expression that is evaluated to provide the values to be transferred, and it specifies the destination to which those values are to be sent. An accept action usage specifies the type of values that can be received by the action. When a send action performed in the source is matched with a compatible accept action performed in the destination, then the transfer of values from the origin to the destination can be completed.<ul><li><p>See the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Send action" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Send action]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> page.</p></li><li><p>See the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Accept action" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Accept action]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> page.</p></li></ul></li></ul>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=254422579 space=SYSML2P version=2 -->
## PAGE 00448: (2026x) Transition

- page_id: `254422579`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/254422579/2026x+Transition
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-10-14T15:23:12.246+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) Model composition > (2026x) Behavior > (2026x) States
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

State usages can be connected by transition usages, which can activate and deactivate the state usages. The triggering of a transition usage from its source state usage to its target state usage deactivates the source state and activates the target state. The trigger of a transition usage is an accept action usage, which accepts an incoming transfer. Transitions may also have guard conditions and effect actions.

##### Creating transitions

###### Creating transitions via the Textual Editor

To create transitions via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, within the body of a state, declare one of the following:
  1. Full transition declaration:
    1. Declare the transition keyword , specify the element name (optional), follow with the keyword first and the source state name, then the keyword then and the target state name.
  2. Shorthand transition declaration:
    1. Declare the keyword then followed by the target state name. For shorthand notation, the keyword *then* must follow after the transition's source state declaration, guard condition, accept action, and effect action.The transition's source state must be a lexically previous state declaration.
2. Click the Synchronize button.

// ------------ Full transition declaration

statedefVehicleStates{ 
entry; 
thenoff; 
stateoff; 
transitionoff_to_startingfirstoffthenstarting; 
statestarting; 
transitionstarting_to_onfirststartingthenon; 
stateon; 
transitionon_to_offfirstonthenoff; // named transition 
// OR 
transitionfirstonthenoff; // unnamed transition 
}

// ------------Shorthand transition declaration

statedefVehicleStates{ 
entry; 
thenoff; 
stateoff; 
thenstarting; // this transition's source is the lexically previous state 'off' 
statestarting; 
thenon; // this transition's source is the lexically previous state 'starting' 
stateon; 
thenoff; // this transition's source is the lexically previous state 'on' 
}

###### Creating transitions via the smart manipulator in a view

To create transitions via the smart manipulator in a view

1. In a view, select a state symbol, and in its smart manipulator, click the transition command.
2. Click on the target element. A transition usage is created between the elements.
3. Specify the transition features.

###### Creating transitions via the view palette

To create transitions via the view palette

1. In the view palette, under the States group, click the **transition** button to create a transition usage.
2. In a view, click on the source element symbol, then click on the target element symbol. A transition usage is created between the elements.
3. Specify the transition features.

##### Specifying transitions

###### Specifying accepters for transitions via the Textual Editor

A transition usage can also have an accepter, which is an accept action usage used to trigger the transition. It may have payload and receiver parameters, accepting the transfer of a payload received by the given receiver.A payload parameter can also include a feature value to only accept the value that is the result of the feature value expression.

To specify accepters for transitions via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, place the cursor between the transition's source and target, before the guard condition, then declare the accept keyword. Transition usages from the entry action are not allowed to have accepters.
2. Follow the keyword with any of the needed options:
  1. Specify a payload parameter.
    1. Specify a feature value of a payload parameter:
      1. A change trigger with the keyword when followed by an expression whose result must be a Boolean value.
      2. An absolute time trigger with the keyword at followed by an expression whose result must be a TimeInstantValue.
      3. A relative time trigger with the keyword after followed by an expression whose result must be a DurationValue.
  2. Identify a transfer receiver parameter with the keyword via . Accepting contextWhen an accept action usage is a composite feature of a part definition or usage, then the default for the receiver (*via*) of the accept action usage is the containing part, not the accept action itself.Otherwise, the accepting context is the highest level containing action usage.When accepting through a port, the port usage is the receiver (*via*).
3. Click the Synchronize button.

transitionon_offfirstonacceptTurnOffviacommPortthenoff; // unnamed payload parameter with the transfer receiver parameter 
transitionon_offfirstonacceptturnOff:TurnOffviacommPortthenoff; // named payload parameter with the transfer receiver parameter 
transitionon_offfirstonacceptTurnOffwhennormalTemp>maxTempthenoff;// change trigger feature value of a payload parametertransitionon_offfirstonacceptTurnOffatmaintenanceTimethen off;// absolute time trigger feature value of a payload parametertransitionon_offfirstonacceptTurnOffafter50[min]thenoff;// relative time trigger feature value of a payload parameter

###### Specifying guard conditions for transitions via the Textual Editor

The****transition usage can contain a guard condition, which is a Boolean expression that must evaluate to *true*for the transition to fire.

To specify guard conditions for transitions via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, place the cursor between the transition's source and target, after the accepter, then declare the needed keyword:
  1. *if* - the specified guard expression must evaluate to *true* for the transition to occur.
  2. if not - the specified guard expression must evaluate to false for the transition to occur.
2. Follow the keyword with the guard expression.
3. Click the Synchronize button.

transitioninitifisInitOffthenoff; // 'if isInitOff' specifies a guard condition which must evaluate to true for the transition to occur 
transitioninitifnotisInitOffthenon; // 'if not isInitOff' specifies a guard condition which must evaluate to false for the transition to occur

###### Specifying effect actions for transitions via the Textual Editor

A transition usage can have an effect action, which is performed if the transition usage is triggered. An effect action is notated using the keyword *do* in the same way as a do action on a state definition or usage.

To specify effect actions for transitions via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, place the cursor between the transition's source and target, after the accepter and guard condition, then declare the do keyword, followed by the needed action.
2. Click the Synchronize button.

transitionoff_onfirstoffacceptTurnOnviacommPortifisEnableddoactionpowerUp:PowerUpthenon; // action usage as the *do*effect action 
transition on_off first on accept TurnOn after 5 [s] if isEnabled do send TimeoutSignal() via commPort then off; // send action usage as the *do send*effect action

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>State usages can be connected by transition usages, which can activate and deactivate the state usages. The triggering of a transition usage from its source state usage to its target state usage deactivates the source state and activates the target state. The trigger of a transition usage is an accept action usage, which accepts an incoming transfer. Transitions may also have guard conditions and effect actions.</p><h3>Creating transitions</h3><h4>Creating transitions via the Textual Editor</h4><p>To create transitions via the Textual name.</li></ol></li><li>Shorthand transition declaration: <ol><li>Declare the keyword <em>then</em> followed by the target state name.<br /><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1"
````
