the view palette</h4><p>To specify accept actions for actions via the view palette</p><hr /><ol><li>In the view palette, under the Other Actions group, click the <strong>accept </strong>button to create an accept action usage element.</li><li>Click on an action symbol in the view pane where you want to create the accept symbol.</li><li>(Optional)<span> </span>Specify the element's parameters via the Textual Editor.</li></ol><h4>Specifying the payload for accept actions via the Drag and Drop handler in a view</h4><p>To specify the payload for accept actions via the Drag and Drop handler in a view</p><hr /><ul><li data-uuid="e5a13364-69f8-44d3-8753-76e530f6a81e">Drag and drop an element from the Containment tree or use its symbol's <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Drag and Drop handler" /></ac:link> on an accept action element symbol executing the <strong>Set Payload </strong>command.</li></ul><h4><span>Specifying the payload for accept actions via the Specification panel</span></h4><p>To specify the payload for accept actions via the Specification panel</p><hr /><ul><li data-uuid="3771bcc7-819c-4e57-8e24-a70d1f0b669d">Drag and drop an element from the Containment tree or use its symbol's <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Drag and Drop handler" /></ac:link> and drop it on the<span> <strong>Payload</strong></span><strong><span> </span></strong>field in the Properties section of the Specification panel for the accept action element.</li></ul>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249577611 space=SYSML2P version=5 -->
## PAGE 00470: Action decomposition

- page_id: `249577611`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/249577611/Action+decomposition
- version_number: 5
- version_date: `2026-04-20T12:56:59.030+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > Model composition > Behavior > Actions
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Action definitions and action usages can be decomposed into lower-level actions to create an action tree. You can define an action usage by an action definition, and an action usage can be subsetted or redefined. This provides enhanced flexibility to modify a hierarchy of action usages to adapt to its context.

##### Decomposing action definitions

You can represent the next level of decomposition of an action definition using the feature typing (*defined by*) relationship.

###### Decomposing action definitions via the Textual Editor

To decompose action definitions via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, identify the action usage that you want to define, nested within an action definition.
2. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Feature typing' space=''] .

actiondefDelivery{ 
actionloadCargo:LoadCargo;// action usage, owned by an action definition, defined by another action definition 
actiontakeOff:TakeOff; 
actiondeliverCargo:DeliverCargo; 
} 
actiondefLoadCargo; 
actiondefTakeOff; 
actiondefDeliverCargo;

###### Displaying decompositions of action definitions in a view

To display decompositions of action definitions in a view

- In a view, select the action definition symbol and the action usage symbol it defines, then use the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Displaying elements in symbolic views' space=''] command. The feature typing relationship is displayed.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Decomposing action usages

You can display an action tree representing a composition of an action from other actions.

###### Decomposing action usages via the Textual Editor

To decompose action usages via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, within the body of the action you want to decompose, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating actions' space=''] .
2. Click the Synchronize button.

action'package delivery':Delivery{ // the owning action usage with nested action usages 
actionloadPackage:LoadCargo; 
actiontakeOff:TakeOff; 
actiondeliverPackge:DeliverCargo; 
}

###### Decomposing action usages via the view palette by creating a composite action

To decompose action usages via the view palette by creating a composite action

1. In a view, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating actions' space=''] or [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Displaying elements in symbolic views' space=''] the action for which you want to create a composite action.
2. Create a composite action in one of the following ways:
  1. Via the view palette (action symbol option):
    1. In the view palette, under the Actions group, click the action button to create an action usage element.
    2. Click on the action symbol displayed in the view pane to create an action owned by another.
  2. Via the view palette (feature membership option):
    1. In the view palette, under the Other Relationships group, click the feature membership button.
    2. First, click on the action symbol displayed in the view pane and then click anywhere in the view pane to create an action owned by another.

###### Decomposing action usages via the view palette by designating an existing action as a composite one

To decompose action usages via the view palette by designating an existing action as a composite one

1. In a view, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating actions' space=''] or [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Displaying elements in symbolic views' space=''] the actions you want to designate as the owner and the owned.
2. In the view palette, under the Other Relationships group, click the feature membership button.
3. First, click on the action symbol you want to designate as the owner, then the one you want to designate as the owned one.

###### Displaying decompositions of action usages in a view

To display decompositions of action usages in a view

- In a view, select the owning action usage symbol and use the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Displaying elements in symbolic views' space=''] command under the Tree-View Style category. The feature membership and the owned features (action usages) are displayed.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Reusing and modifying the action tree

An action can subset another action, facilitating action tree reuse and modification. The subsetting action inherits the action decomposition and other features. You can then modify the inherited actions and features through redefinition and subsetting in addition to adding new actions.

###### Reusing and modifying the actions tree via the Textual Editor

To reuse and modify the actions tree via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating actions' space=''] you want to reuse, then [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Subsetting' space=''] it.
2. (Optional) [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Redefinition' space=''] or [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Subsetting' space=''] the action features in the element body.
3. Click the Synchronize button.

action'package delivery' :Delivery{// subsetted action 
actionloadPackage:LoadCargo; 
actiontakeOff:TakeOff; 
actiondeliverPackge:DeliverCargo; 
} 
action'heater delivery':>'package delivery'{// an action reuses the actions tree by subsetting another action 
action:>>loadPackage; 
action:>>takeOff; 
action:>>deliverPackge; 
}

###### Reusing the actions tree via the view palette

To reuse the actions tree via the view palette

1. In a view, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating actions' space=''] or [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Displaying elements in symbolic views' space=''] the action whose decomposition you want to reuse.
2. In the view palette, under the Specializations group, click the subsetting button.
3. Click on the action you want to denote as the subsetting one and then click on the action you want to denote as the subsetted (reused) one.

###### Displaying the actions tree reuse in a view

To display the actions tree reuse in a view

1. In a view, select the subsetting and the subsetted action usage symbols, then u se the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Displaying elements in symbolic views' space=''] command . The subsetting relationship is displayed.
2. (Optional) To display owned features of the subsetting or subsetted actions, use the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Displaying elements in symbolic views' space=''] command. The owned features (action usages) are displayed. [ATTACHMENT filename='Displaying the actions tree reuse on a diagram.png']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249577544 space=SYSML2P version=2 -->
## PAGE 00471: Action succession and flow

- page_id: `249577544`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/249577544/Action+succession+and+flow
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-12-11T19:47:14.489+01:00`
- ancestors: SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > Model composition > Behavior > Actions
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can connect actions using successions and input/output flows.

50%

Use successions to connect and sequence actions:

- A succession asserts that the target action can start execution only after the source action ends execution.
  - See the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Succession' space=''] page.
- A succession within the body of an action definition or usage may be given a guard condition represented as a Boolean expression, forming a conditional succession . A conditional succession asserts that the target action can start only if the guard condition is true .
  - See the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Guard conditions' space=''] page.

In addition to the regular full notation for successions, you can also use textual notation succession shorthands within the body of an action definition or usage. See the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Succession' space=''] page.

50%

Use the following kinds of input/output flows to connect elements and transfer items from a source output to a target input:

- A flow is a transfer that continues while the source and target actions execute (this is the default)
  - See the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Flow' space=''] page.
- A succession flow is a transfer that only occurs after the source action ends and before the target action starts.
  - See the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Succession flow' space=''] page.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p>You can connect actions using successions and input/output flows.</p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><ac:structured-macro ac:name="section" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f09ce940-fddf-4a32-99d4-b7338bf715fa"><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="59abc492-5f5a-4e86-851d-d7f10948697c"><ac:parameter ac:name="width">50%</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><span>Use successions to connect and sequence actions:</span></p><ul><li data-uuid="d98eb37b-e09c-4d19-b580-083948812726"><span>A </span><strong>succession</strong><span> asserts that the target action can start execution only after the source action ends execution. </span><ul><li><span>See the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Succession" /></ac:link> page.</span></li></ul></li><li data-uuid="d72153cc-6b4b-4c66-865f-b748d5219931">A succession within the body of an action definition or usage may be given a guard condition represented as a Boolean expression, forming a <strong>conditional succession</strong>. A conditional succession asserts that the target action can start only if the guard condition is <em>true</em>.<ul><li>See the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Guard conditions" /></ac:link> page.</li></ul></li></ul><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c724e1f3-91b2-4c79-b12f-6c78400b9010"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span>In addition to the regular full notation for successions, you can also use textual notation succession shorthands within the body of an action definition or usage. See the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Succession" /></ac:link> page.</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c49a3755-fa30-4e39-9740-dd6c338411b7"><ac:parameter ac:name="width">50%</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><span>Use the following kinds of input/output flows to connect elements and transfer items from a source output to a target input: </span></p><ul><li data-uuid="2cad65f7-f5b5-458d-971e-86f961badab8"><span>A </span><strong>flow</strong><span> is a transfer that continues while the source and target actions execute (this is the default) </span><ul><li>See the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Flow" /></ac:link> page.</li></ul></li><li data-uuid="0cb49b43-6394-43b7-8da1-68ca6897b146">A <strong>succession flow </strong>is a transfer that only occurs after the source action ends and before the target action starts.<ul><li>See the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Succession flow" /></ac:link> page.</li></ul></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=280231959 space=SYSML2P version=13 -->
## PAGE 00473: Activating Element Creation dialog

- page_id: `280231959`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/280231959/Activating+Element+Creation+dialog
- version_number: 13
- version_date: `2026-05-06T20:12:04.741+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > Model customization > Custom creation dialogs > Custom Element Creation dialog
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The Active Create Element Dialog allows you to manage all Element Creation dialogs, determining which one should be activated for the project.Thepredefined*SysMLElementCreationDialog*configuration is activated by default for all SysML v2 projects. If you prefer using your custom-created Element Creation dialog, you must activate it via the Active Create Element dialog.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The **Manage Create Element Dialog** command invokes the **Active Create Element Dialog**, where the custom Element Creation dialog's *NewCustomElementCreationDialog* configuration is activated for the project.

##### Activating the Element Creation dialog

Activating an Element Creation dialog for the project via the **Active Create Element Dialog**automatically creates a new package *ProjectElementCreationConfig* with the active Element Creation dialog configuration. The package is created in a root namespace dedicated to configurations that activate [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Applying style sheets' space=''], [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Custom creation dialogs' space=''], and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Enabling validation suites' space='']. If no such namespace exists in the model, it is automatically created with the *ProjectElementCreationConfig*package. The root namespace's name depends on the name of the package first created in the namespace.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

- Only one Element Creation dialog can be activated for a project. If you specify multiple, only the first will be activated.
- If a newly created Element Creation dialog is not visible in the Active Element Creation Dialog , refresh the project by pressing F5 or clicking View > Refresh.

###### Activating the Element Creation dialog via the element shortcut menu

To activate the Element Creation dialog via the element shortcut menu

1. In the Containment tree, right-click the part definition element of an Element Creation dialog configuration.
2. In the shortcut menu, click the Manage Create Element Dialog command. The Active Create Element Dialog opens.
3. On the left side panel of thedialog, double-click the dialog configuration you want to use.
4. Click the**OK**button. The selected dialog configuration is activated.

###### Activating the Element Creation dialog via Project Options

To activate the Element Creation dialog via Project Options

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Project options' space=''] and in the General group, click SysML v2 .
2. In the value column for the Active Element Creation Dialog option, click the Browse button. The Active Create Element Dialog opens.
3. On the left side panel of thedialog, double-click the dialog configuration you want to use.
4. Click the OK button.
5. Click the OK button in the Project Options dialog. The selected dialog configuration is activated.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

The selected dialog configuration is activated.</li></ol>

## PAGE 00474: Activating Relationship Creation dialog

- page_id: `280756320`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/280756320/Activating+Relationship+Creation+dialog
- version_number: 14
- version_date: `2026-05-06T20:12:26.804+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > Model customization > Custom creation dialogs > Custom Relationship Creation dialog
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The Active Create Relationship Dialog allows you to manage all Relationship Creation dialogs, determining which one should be activated for the project.Thepredefined*SysMLRelationshipCreationDialog*configuration is activated by default for all SysML v2 projects. If you prefer using your custom-created Relationship Creation dialog, you must activate it via the Active Create Relationship dialog.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The **Manage Create Relationship Dialog** command invokes the **Active Create Relationship Dialog**, where the custom Relationship Creation dialog's *CustomizedRelationshipCreationDialog* configuration is activated for the project.

##### Activating the Relationship Creation dialog

Activating a Relationship Creation dialog for the project via the **Active Create Relationship Dialog**automatically creates a new package *ProjectRelationCreationConfig* with the active Relationship Creation dialog configuration. The package is created in a root namespace dedicated to configurations that activate [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Applying style sheets' space=''], [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Custom creation dialogs' space=''], and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Enabling validation suites' space='']. If no such namespace exists in the model, it is automatically created with the *ProjectRelationCreationConfig*package. The root namespace's name depends on the name of the package first created in the namespace.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

- Only one Relationship Creation dialog can be activated for a project. If you specify multiple, only the first will be activated.
- If a newly created Relationship Creation dialog is not visible in the Active Relationship Creation Dialog , refresh the project by pressing F5 or clicking View > Refresh.

###### Activating the Relationship Creation dialog via the element shortcut menu

To activate the Relationship Creation dialog via the element shortcut menu

1. In the Containment tree, right-click the part definition element of a Relationship Creation dialog configuration.
2. In the shortcut menu, click the Manage Create Relationship Dialog command. The Active Create Relationship Dialog opens.
3. On the left side panel of thedialog, double-click the dialog configuration you want to use.
4. Click the**OK**button. The selected dialog configuration is activated.

###### Activating the Relationship Creation dialog via Project Options

To activate the Relationship Creation dialog via Project Options

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Project options' space=''] and in the General group, click SysML v2 .
2. In the value column for the Active Relationship Creation Dialog option, click the Browse button. The Active Create Relationship Dialog opens.
3. On the left side panel of thedialog, double-click the dialog configuration you want to use.
4. Click the OK button.
5. Click the OK button in the Project Options dialog. The selected dialog configuration is activated.

## PAGE 00475: Activating View Creation dialog

- page_id: `249577905`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/249577905/Activating+View+Creation+dialog
- version_number: 24
- version_date: `2026-05-06T20:11:31.016+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > Model customization > Custom creation dialogs > Custom View Creation dialog
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The Active Create View Dialog allows you to manage all View Creation dialogs, determining which one should be activated for the project.Thepredefined*SysMLViewCreationDialog*configuration is activated by default for all SysML v2 projects. If you prefer using your custom-created View Creation dialog, you must activate it via the Active Create View dialog.[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The **Manage Create View Dialog** command invokes the **Active Create View Dialog**, where the custom View Creation dialog's *CustomViewCreationDialog* configuration is activated for the project.

##### Activating the View Creation dialog

Activating a View Creation dialog for the project via the **Active Create View Dialog**automatically creates a new package *ProjectViewCreationConfig* with the active View Creation dialog configuration. The package is created in a root namespace dedicated to configurations that activate [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Applying style sheets' space=''], [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Custom creation dialogs' space=''], and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Enabling validation suites' space='']. If no such namespace exists in the model, it is automatically created with the *ProjectViewCreationConfig*package. The root namespace's name depends on the name of the package first created in the namespace.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

- Only one View Creation dialog can be activated for a project. If you specify multiple, only the first will be activated.
- If a newly created View Creation dialog is not visible in the Active Create View Dialog , refresh the project by pressing F5 or clicking View > Refresh.

###### Activating the View Creation dialog via the element shortcut menu

To activate the View Creation dialog via the element shortcut menu

1. In the Containment tree, right-click the part definition element of a View Creation dialog configuration.
2. In the shortcut menu, click the Manage Create View Dialog command. The Active Create View Dialog opens.
3. On the left side panel of thedialog, double-click the dialog configuration you want to use.
4. Click the**OK**button. The selected dialog configuration is activated.

###### Activating the View Creation dialog via Project Options

To activate the View Creation dialog via Project Options

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Project options' space=''] and in the General group, click SysML v2 .
2. In the value column for the Active View Creation Dialog option, click the Browse button. The Active Create View Dialog opens.
3. On the left side panel of thedialog, double-click the dialog configuration you want to use.
4. Click the**OK**button.
5. Click the OK button in the Project Options dialog. The selected dialog configuration is activated.

````html
<p>The Active Create View Dialog allows you to manage all View Creation dialogs, <span data-ref="c1b57145-b21f-4516-86ee-0993b2a03233">determining which one should be activated for the project. </span><span data-ref="c1b57145-b21f-4516-86ee-0993b2a03233">The</span><span data-ref="c1b57145-b21f-4516-86ee-0993b2a03233"> predefined </span><em><span data-ref="c1b57145-b21f-4516-86ee-0993b2a03233">SysMLViewCreationDialog </span></em><span data-ref="c1b57145-b21f-4516-86ee-0993b2a03233">configuration</span> is activated by default for all SysML v2 projects. If you prefer using your custom-created View Creation dialog, you must activate it via the Active Create View dialog.<ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="View Creation Dialog Management.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The <strong>Manage Create View Dialog</strong> command invokes the <strong>Active Create View Dialog</strong>, where the custom View Creation dialog's <em>CustomViewCreationDialog</em> configuration is activated for the project.</h6><h3>Activating the View Creation dialog</h3><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8268cf0d-b191-4a98-9689-dcd5c5d2e11f"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Activating a View Creation dialog for the project via the <strong>Active Create View Dialog </strong>automatically creates a new package <em>ProjectViewCreationConfig</em> with the active View Creation dialog configuration. The package is created in a root namespace dedicated to configurations that activate <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Applying style sheets" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[style sheets]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Custom creation dialogs" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[creation dialogs]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, and <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Enabling validation suites" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[validation suites]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>. If no such namespace exists in the model, it is automatically created with the <em>ProjectViewCreationConfig </em>package. The root namespace's name depends on the name of the package first created in the namespace. </p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Active Create View Dialog configuration.png" /></ac:image></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="99cd784b-d6c0-432d-aaf2-145e5f75191e"><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li data-uuid="7d426d63-d399-4076-92b3-523debc52b3e">Only one View Creation dialog can be activated for a project. If you specify multiple, only the first will be activated.</li><li data-uuid="fbdcc1e6-32f9-46dc-b3cc-f160f93dcda9">If a newly created View Creation dialog is not visible in the <strong>Active Create View Dialog</strong>, refresh the project by pressing <strong>F5 </strong>or clicking <strong>View &gt; Refresh.</strong></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h4>Activating the View Creation dialog via the element shortcut menu</h4><p>To activate the View Creation dialog via the element shortcut menu</p><hr /><ol><li data-uuid="c15158da-a1e8-446b-9cca-106547b0acae">In the Containment tree, right-click the part definition element of a View Creation dialog configuration.</li><li data-uuid="dc4c3ce6-dcfc-4a45-9554-9cd8a6343744">In the shortcut menu, click the <strong>Manage Create View Dialog </strong>command. The <strong>Active Create View Dialog </strong>opens.</li><li data-uuid="fe7cb699-b8a7-4f5f-bb8e-59989cc923d5"><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-magenta-bolder,#50253f);"><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-magenta-bolder,#50253f);"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">On the left side panel of the<span> </span></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">dialog, double-click the dialog configuration you want to use. </span></span></span></li><li data-uuid="fe7cb699-b8a7-4f5f-bb8e-59989cc923d5"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Click the<span> </span></span><strong style="text-align: left;">OK</strong><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);"><span> </span>button. The selected dialog configuration is activated</span>.<br style="text-align: left;" /></span></span></li></ol><h4>Activating the View Creation dialog via Project Options</h4><p>To activate the View Creation dialog via Project Options</p><hr /><ol><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Project options" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Open the Project Options dialog]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> and in the <strong>General </strong>group, click <strong>SysML v2</strong>.</li><li data-uuid="f33b2b91-3c1a-4787-9a02-b2543c3faa1f">In the value column for the <strong>Active View Creation Dialog </strong>option, click the <strong>Browse </strong>button. The <strong>Active Create View Dialog </strong>opens.</li><li data-uuid="41e45714-1bd6-455d-9e61-832643fa1a2b"><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-magenta-bolder,#50253f);"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">On the left side panel of the<span> </span></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">dialog, double-click the dialog configuration you want to use. </span></span></li><li data-uuid="69f2fd85-0cb8-4a85-8dad-5a9f16b34132"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Click the<span> </span></span><strong style="text-align: left;">OK</strong><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);"><span> </span>button. </span></span></li><li data-uuid="134d5698-9745-4269-bb26-d12cfe7bc54c">Click the <strong>OK </strong>button in the <strong>Project Options </strong>dialog. The selected dialog configuration is activated.</li></ol>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249577209 space=SYSML2P version=2 -->
## PAGE 00476: Alias

- page_id: `249577209`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/249577209/Alias
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2026-06-16T15:17:52.433+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > Element management > Working with elements
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can specify aliases for elements within the context of a namespace. The elements may be owned members of the namespace or individual members of other packages. The alias name or short name can be different than the element's declared name or declared short name.

##### Specifying an alias

###### Specifying an alias via the Textual Editor

To specify an alias via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, place the cursor where you want to create the alias, declare the keyword alias , followed by the alias short name and/or alias name, followed by the keyword for, and the name of the element for which you want to specify an alias.
2. (Optional) [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Annotations' space='']
3. Click the Synchronize button.

package'Automobile'{ 
publicimportISQ::TorqueValue; 
privateimportScalarValues::*; 
partdefAutomobile; 
aliasCarforAutomobile{ 
doc/* Documentation of the alias. */// alias with documentation 
} 
alias<trq>TorqueforISQ::TorqueValue;// declared alias short name '<trq>' and declared alias name 'Torque' 
}

You can control the display of the alias name and short name in element symbols through the **Show Alias Name**and **Show Alias Short Name**[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Modifying styles of individual symbols' space=''].

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249577354 space=SYSML2P version=8 -->
## PAGE 00477: Allocation

- page_id: `249577354`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/249577354/Allocation
- version_number: 8
- version_date: `2025-10-07T13:07:12.776+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > Model composition > Relations > Traceability
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

An allocation definition is a connection definition that specifies that some or all of the responsibility for realizing the intent of the source is allocated to the target, defining traceable links as a kind of mapping across the various structures and hierarchies of a system model, possibly prior to more rigorous specification. An allocation usage must allocate features that conform to the types of the ends of its allocation definition.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The image displays a *torqueGenAlloc* allocation usage defined by the *LogicalToPhysical*allocation definition. The allocation allocates the *torqueGenerator*part usage to the *powerTrain* part usage. 
Additionally, the allocation connector is also [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Displaying elements in symbolic views' space=''].

##### Creating allocation usage and definition elements

###### Creating allocation usage elements via the Textual Editor

To create allocation usage elements via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, place the cursor where you want to create the element and do one of the following:
  1. For an unnamed and unspecialized allocation usage, use the allocate keyword, followed by explicit related feature declarations or a comma-separated list of related features.
  2. For a named and/or specialized allocation usage, use the allocation keyword, specify the element name (optional), [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specializations' space=''] (optional), then the allocate keyword, followed by explicit related feature declarations or a comma-separated list of related features.
2. Click the Synchronize button. allocatetorqueGeneratortopowerTrain; // unnamed unspecialized allocation usage 
allocate(torqueGenerator,powerTrain); // unnamed unspecialized allocation usage allocating elements in a comma-separated listallocation : LogicalToPhysical allocate torqueGenerator to powerTrain; // unnamed specialized (feature typing) allocation usageallocationtorqueGenAllocallocatetorqueGeneratortopowerTrain; // named unspecialized allocation usageallocationtorqueGenAlloc:LogicalToPhysicalallocatetorqueGeneratortopowerTrain; // named specialized (feature typing) allocation usage// unnamed unspecialized allocation usage refined using a nested allocation for a more detailed decomposition of the containing allocation mapping: 
allocatetorqueGeneratortopowerTrain{ 
allocatetorqueGenerator.generateTorquetopowerTrain.engine.generateTorque; 
}

###### Creating allocation usage elements via the Create Relation command

To create allocation usage elements via the Create Relation command

1. Right-click the element you want to allocate.
2. In the shortcut menu, click Create Relation , then in the submenu, under Outgoing , click allocation . The Create... dialog opens.
3. In the Create... dialog, do one of the following:
  1. In a view, click the element symbol you want to allocate. Hold Shift to select multiple elements.
  2. Click the Add [IMAGE alt='' src=''] button. The element selection dialog opens. In the element selection dialog, select the element you want to allocate to and click OK .
  3. Drag and drop the needed element you want to allocate to from the Containment tree onto the Create... dialog.
4. In the Create... dialog, click OK .

###### Creating allocation definition and usage elements via the view palette

To create allocation definition and usage elements via the view palette

- To create an allocation definition:
  1. In the view palette, under the Connectors group, click the **allocation** def button to create an allocation definition element.
  2. Click in the view pane where you want to create the allocation definition symbol.
- To create an allocation usage:
  1. In the view palette, under the Connectors group, click the **allocation** button to create an allocation usage.
  2. In a view, click on the element symbol you want to allocate, then on the element you want to allocate to. An allocation connection symbol is created between the elements. If the needed button is not displayed, click the down-facing arrow next to either the **allocation**or the **allocation****def**button and select the needed button in the dropdown menu.

###### Creating allocation definition elements via the Textual Editor

To create allocation definition elements via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, place the cursor where you want to create the element and declare the allocation def keyword for an allocation definition.
2. Specify the element name, then open curly braces and specify the allocation definition features within the braces:
  1. Specify the allocation definition ends by declaring the end keyword, followed by the end name and/or [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specializations' space=''] of the needed element.
3. Click the Synchronize button. allocationdefLogicalToPhysical{ 
endlogical:LogicalElement; 
endphysical:PhysicalElement; 
}

###### Creating allocation definition elements via the Create Element command

To create allocation definition elements via the Create Element command

1. In the Containment tree, right-click an element, and in the shortcut menu, click **Create Element**.
2. In the Create Element dialog, click allocation def for allocation definition.

###### Auto-creating allocation definition elements via the Extract Definition commands

To auto-create an allocation definition element via the Extract Definition commands

- See the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Extract Definition and Usage' space='']page.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p><span>An allocation definition is a connection definition that specifies that some or all of the responsibility for realizing the intent of the source is allocated to the target, defining traceable links as a kind of mapping across the various structures and hierarchies of a system model, possibly prior to more rigorous specification. An allocation usage must allocate features that conform to the types of the ends of its allocation definition.</span></p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="display_allocation.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><span>The image displays a <em>torqueGenAlloc</em> allocation usage defined by the <em>LogicalToPhysical </em>allocation definition. style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> {         </span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">allocate</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">torqueGenerator</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">.</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">generateTorque</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">to</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">powerTrain</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">.</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">engine</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">.</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">generateTorque</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">;</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">}</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li></ol><h4>Creating allocation usage elements <span>via the Create Relation command</span></h4><p>To create allocation usage elements via the Create Relation command</p><hr /><ol><li>Right-click the element you want to allocate.</li><li>In the shortcut menu, click <strong>Create Relation</strong>, then in the submenu, under <strong>Outgoing</strong>, click <strong>allocation</strong>. The <strong style="text-align: left;">Create...</strong><strong> </strong>dialog opens.</li><li>In the <strong style="text-align: left;">Create...</strong><strong> </strong>dialog, do one of the following:<ol><li>In a view, click the element symbol you want to allocate. Hold Shift to select multiple elements. </li><li>Click the <strong>Add <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="add_button.png" /></ac:image> </strong>button. The element selection dialog opens. In the element selection dialog, select the element you want to allocate to and click <strong>OK</strong>.</li><li>Drag and drop the needed element you want to allocate to from the Containment tree onto the <strong style="text-align: left;">Create... </strong>dialog.</li></ol></li><li>In the <strong style="text-align: left;">Create...</strong><strong> </strong>dialog, click <strong>OK</strong>. </li></ol><h4>Creating allocation definition and usage elements via the view palette</h4><p>To create allocation definition and usage elements via the view palette</p><hr /><ul><li>To create an allocation definition:<ol><li>In the view palette, under the Connectors group, click the<span> <strong>allocation </strong></span><strong>def </strong><span>button to create an allocation definition element.</span></li><li>Click in the view pane where you want to create the allocation definition symbol.</li></ol></li><li>To create an allocation usage:<ol><li>In the view palette, under the Connectors group, click the<span> <strong>allocation </strong></span><span>button to create an allocation usage.</span></li><li>In a view, click on the element symbol you want to allocate, then on the element you want to allocate to. An allocation connection symbol is created between the elements.<ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="769c3a3a-7b27-471d-b151-165748c95ab6"><ac:rich-text-body><p>If the needed button is not displayed, click the down-facing arrow next to either the <span><strong>allocation </strong></span>or the <span><strong>allocation </strong></span><strong>def </strong>button and select the needed button in the dropdown menu.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li></ol></li></ul><h4>Creating allocation definition elements via the Textual Editor</h4><p>To create allocation definition elements via the <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Textual Editor</span></p><hr /><ol><li>In the Textual Editor, place the cursor where you want to create the element and declare the<span> </span><em>allocation def</em><span> keyword </span><span>for an allocation definition.</span></li><li>Specify the element name, then open curly braces and specify the allocation definition features within the braces:<ol><li>Specify the allocation definition ends by declaring the <em>end</em> keyword, followed by the end name and/or <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Specializations" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[specialization]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> of the needed element. </li></ol></li><li>Click the <strong>Synchronize </strong>button.<br /><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c00fd817-e309-4d8e-839d-5b21596e9da9"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">allocation</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">def</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">LogicalToPhysical</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> {</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">end</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">logical</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> : </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">LogicalElement</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">;</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">end</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">physical</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> : </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">PhysicalElement</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">;</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">}</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li></ol><h4>Creating allocation definition elements via the Create Element command</h4><p>To create allocation definition elements via the Create Element command</p><hr /><ol><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">In the Containment tree, right-click an element, and in the shortcut menu, click <strong>Create Element</strong>.</p></li><li>In the <strong>Create Element</strong> dialog, click <strong>allocation def</strong> for allocation definition.</li></ol><h4>Auto-creating allocation definition elements via the Extract Definition commands</h4><p>To auto-create an allocation definition element via the Extract Definition commands</p><hr /><ul><li><span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">See the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Extract Definition and Usage" /></ac:link></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">page.</span></span></li></ul>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249576712 space=SYSML2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 00478: Alpha 1.3 Version News

- page_id: `249576712`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/249576712/Alpha+1.3+Version+News
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-08-19T12:12:10.627+02:00`
- ancestors: 
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

###### SysML v2 Plugin

Released on: February 6, 2024

The SysML v2 Alpha 1.3 release is a technology preview allowing you to evaluate some of the already implemented SysML v2 features.

#### N-ary Interface and Connection Support

The Alpha 1.3 version introduces the ability to create and display n-ary interfaces and connections in diagrams.

*CANBusInterfaces*in a vehicle context is a basic example of modeling these. The *CANBusInterface*can be modeled as an n-ary interface, interconnecting multiple subsystems, each equipped with a *CANBusPort*, enabling multipoint communications.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']The *vehicleCANBus*n-ary interface is connected to multiple part elements.

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='N-ary connection' space='']>]]>

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='N-ary interface' space='']>]]>

#### Metadata Implementation

The Alpha 1.3 version introduces a way to annotate elements with metadata. As with stereotypes in SysML v1, metadata allows extending model elements by adding various attributes, semantics, and notation. It can be used to customize various models for specific domains or purposes.

For example, you can create a stereotype for a requirement to add additional attributes. In the following example, the *vehicleMassRequirement*element is annotated with the ‘Status Info’ metadata, which provides information such as status, originator, and owner. Furthermore, the risk level can be specified for each requirement with possible values (high, medium, or low).

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']The *StatusInfo* metadata is applied to an element.

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Metadata' space='']>]]>

#### Model-Based Styles Implementation Basics

Basic symbol style functionality has been implemented in the 1.3 release. Now, shapes and paths on diagrams have a basic set of style attributes that govern the appearance of symbols that can be set by the user. Specifying the symbol styles *en masse* using a model-based rule-governed style mechanism is also possible.

Style functionality is a work in progress and will be developed further in subsequent Alpha releases. The most important missing piece is the mechanism for defining conditions for rule-based style applications. Compartment support will also be added, as well as a richer style attribute set and a nice UI for specifying style rules.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']Default symbol styles vs those customized via the Symbol Properties dialog.

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Symbol styles' space='']>]]>

#### Model Patch Mechanism

The Alpha 1.3 version introduces the Model Patch mechanism. This lightweight approach enables you to:

- Effortlessly transfer SysML v2 elements or their changes between projects, fostering seamless collaboration across teams. It simplifies model reuse and promotes efficient model evolution.
- Target specific model modifications for sharing with disconnected teams or integrating into other projects, eliminating the need to distribute entire models. It fosters focused collaboration while minimizing the set of exchanged data.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### Evaluation and Requirements Verification in a Diagram

The SysML v2 Evaluation plugin version Alpha 1.3 now introduces the ability to display evaluation results as annotations directly within diagrams. This update also includes a recursive check of requirements, simplifying the process of verifying whether a system meets declared requirements and/or constraints.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**Documentation**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML v2 Alpha 1.3' space='SYSML2PAlpha13']

It simplifies model reuse and promotes efficient model evolution.</li><li>Target specific model modifications for sharing with disconnected teams or integrating into other projects, eliminating the need to distribute entire models. <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249576690 space=SYSML2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 00479: Alpha 1.4 Version News

- page_id: `249576690`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/249576690/Alpha+1.4+Version+News
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-08-19T12:11:14.702+02:00`
- ancestors: 
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

###### SysML v2 Plugin

Released on:May 13, 2024

The SysML v2 Alpha 1.4 release is a technology preview that will allow you to evaluate some of the SysML v2 features that have been implemented.

#### Individuals

The Alpha 1.4 release introduces the capability to designate elements as individuals and apply timeslices to them, enhancing the modeling framework. Similar to the concept of instances in SysML v1, the individuals feature enables the explicit representation of a distinct entity within a modeled system, allowing for its full or partial characterization. These entities are tangible or conceptual items that possess a distinct identity.

For example, the part definition *Car*represents the category encompassing all cars. An individual car, uniquely named *MyCar* and identified by a specific vehicle identification number, is depicted as an individual part definition. This definition acts as a specialized subclassification of the broader part definition *Car*, thereby defining that particular vehicle’s unique characteristics.

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Occurrences' space='']>]]>

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### Specification window

The Alpha 1.4 version introduces the newly implemented ability to open a Specification window for any element in the project. Currently, the Specification window is read-only, allowing you to filter element properties and values as well as sort the properties alphabetically in ascending or descending order. Property editing is expected to be available in future releases.

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='']>]]>

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### Swimlanes

Swimlanes are an essential view for indicating which structural parts are responsible for performing specific actions. Appearing within the perform action element, swimlanes represent the subparts of the perform action context, where each subpart is marked with the «performer» keyword. The parts responsibility for actions is reflected by performing them.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### Model-Based Styles

SysML v2 Alpha 1.4 introduces the following enhancements regarding model-based styles:

- The Symbol Properties dialog has been supplemented with compartment-based groups, such as documentation, subject, objectives, etc. Every group contains properties that control the corresponding compartment of the selected symbol. The availability of each compartment-based group depends on the compartments available to the specific symbol's element kind. The following image showcases the vehicleSpecification requirement usage symbol containing the documentation, subject, and require constraints compartments. The Symbol Properties dialog invoked for this symbol displays all of the compartment-based groups and their properties available to the requirement usage element symbol. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
- The Alpha 1.4 release introduces the 'Symbol Styles' library that contains a stylesheet comprised of a set of rules defining symbol styles based on their element kind. You can review the SysML v2 project samples provided with the installation, which display all element symbols with specific styles based on their element kind. 
 
The following image displays green-colored part usage element symbols, whose style is defined based on their element kind.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Symbol styles' space='']>]]>

#### Other Improvements

- With Alpha 1.4, there is a single SysML v2 project template with the 'Standard' and 'Symbol Styles' libraries used in projects rather than embedded. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
- You can now subset features using the convenient new drag and drop command Create Subsetting Usage , available when dragging and dropping a usage element symbol on any other element symbol. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Drag and Drop handler' space='']>]]> [ATTACHMENT filename='create_subsetting_usage.png']
- Now you can easily redefine inherited features via the Redefine command available in the inherited element symbol's shortcut menu. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specializations' space='']>]]> [ATTACHMENT filename='redefine_command.png']

**Documentation**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML v2 Alpha 1.4' space='SYSML2PAlpha14']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

element symbol on any other element symbol. </span><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Drag and Drop handler" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Learn more >>]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link><br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="create_subsetting_usage.png" /></ac:image></li><li>Now you can easily redefine inherited features via the <strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Redefine</strong><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> command available in the inherited element symbol's shortcut menu. </span><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Specializations" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Learn more >>]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="redefine_command.png" /></ac:image></li></ul><p style="text-align: right;"><ac:link ac:anchor="top"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Back to top]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p><hr /></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Documentation</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="SYSML2PAlpha14" ri:content-title="SysML v2 Alpha 1.4" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249576645 space=SYSML2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 00480: Alpha 1.5 Version News

- page_id: `249576645`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/249576645/Alpha+1.5+Version+News
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-15T09:45:49.165+02:00`
- ancestors: 
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

###### SysML v2 Plugin

Released on:October 8, 2024

The SysML v2 Alpha 1.5 release is a technology preview that will allow you to evaluate some of the SysML v2 features that have been implemented.

- 
- 
- 

#### Full Support for SysML v2 in the Textual Editor

The SysML v2 Textual Editor now fully supports all concepts of the SysML v2 modeling language, providing 100% coverage.

Explore every aspect of SysML v2 with confidence, as our editor meets all your modeling needs — from requirements, structure, and behavior to analysis cases, verification cases, and more.

Please note, filter conditions are not applicable for views.

#### Rule-Based Styles

The Alpha 1.5 version introduces new and improved rule-based styles functionality, including:

- Predefined rule-based styles . Now the Symbol Styles library comes with two predefined style sheets: the improved DefaultStyle style sheet with updated rules and styles as well as the new WhiteAndBlackStyle style sheet that contains rule-based styles for representing all element symbols in black and white style. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Predefined style sheets' space='']>]]> [ATTACHMENT filename='predefined_rule_based_styles.png']

###### Symbol styles represented by the *DefaultStyle*(left) and *WhiteAndBlackStyle*(right) style sheets.

- Custom rule-based styles . Instead of using the predefined styles, you can now create style sheets with your own rule-based styles either by reusing and customizing the *DefaultStyle* style sheet or creating a custom style sheet from scratch. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Custom style sheets' space='']>]]> [IMAGE alt='' src='']The predefined rule-based symbol styles using the *DefaultStyle*style sheet (left) and an example of custom rule-based symbol styles using a custom-created style sheet (right).
- Layout-sensitive changes assessment . The Symbol Properties dialog has been enhanced to track style attribute changes that can affect already-created diagrams' layout. Easily assess the highlighted properties and either confirm or deny the changes. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Assessing layout-sensitive changes' space='']>]]> [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
The Symbol Properties dialog displays the highlighted rows of layout-sensitive attributes whose values were updated in the style sheet. 
At the top of the dialog, a notification provides commands for either using the new style or retaining the old one. 
The image displays the updated *driver*part usage element symbol once the 'Use the new style' command is selected in the dialog notification.
- Style sheets management . The Active Style Sheets dialog allows you to manage all existing style sheets, both predefined and custom, determining which one(s) should be activated for the project. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Applying style sheets' space='']>]]> 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Activating the *WhiteAndBlackStyle*style sheet instead of the *DefaultStyle*via the Active Style Sheets dialog.

#### Specification Pane

The Alpha 1.5 version introduces the Specification pane that can be opened for any element in the project. It allows you to review and modify properties of specific elements, such as the declared name, declared short name, type, value, and documentation. You can also review, modify, or createowned elements in the appropriate compartments, which are equivalent to those displayed in diagram symbols.[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification panel' space='']>]]>

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
The Specification pane displays the *vehicleMassLimitationRequirement*element's specification information.

#### States

The new tool version now supports state modeling, enhancing the ability to accurately represent dynamic system behavior and improving clarity and consistency in behavioral modeling. This includes:

- State modeling , enabling the creation of state usage and state definition elements, with support for special actions like entry, do, and exit actions. Learn more [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating states' space='']>]]> and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Entry, do, and exit actions for states' space='']>]]>
- Parallel state specification for modeling concurrent behaviors. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Parallel states' space='']>]]>
- State transitions , allowing you to specify movement between states with the ability to add triggers, guard conditions, and effect actions. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Transition' space='']>]]>
- Exhibit state specification , which lets you specify elements that exhibit specific states. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Exhibit state' space='']>]]>

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']The image displays a *Vehicle* part which exhibits the *operationalStates*state machine. The states include special actions like entry, do, and exit actions. The states are connected via transitions, which also contain triggers, guard conditions and effect actions.

#### Allocations

The new release allows you to create allocations where the responsibility for realizing the intent of the source is allocated to the target, defining traceable links as a kind of mapping across the various structures and hierarchies of a system model.

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Allocation' space='']>]]>

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The image displays a *torqueGenAlloc* allocation usage feature typed by the *LogicalToPhysical*allocation definition. The allocation allocates the *torqueGenerator*part usage to the *powerTrain* part usage. Additionally, the allocation connector is also [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Displaying elements in symbolic views' space=''].

#### Displaying Relationships

The Display commands now include two new categories — Relationships and In-Between Relationships — for displaying relationships between element symbols on a diagram. The Relationships category contains commands for displaying the relationship paths between the selected symbol and all other symbols displayed on the diagram. The In-Between Relationships category contains commands for displaying relationship paths only between the selected symbols. The commands in these categories allow you to display all types of connectors, specializations, and other relationships (e.g., annotations, memberships, etc.).

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Displaying elements in symbolic views' space='']>]]>

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### On the left: the 'Display Other' command from the In-Between Relationships display category is executed for two symbols selected on the diagram, displaying the relationship (feature membership)between them.On the right: the 'Display Other' command from the Relationships display category is executed for the selected symbol, displaying its relationships (feature memberships) with other symbols displayed on the diagram.

#### Copying and Pasting Elements

Now you can copy, paste, cut, and delete elements using the appropriate commands in the element shortcut menu in the Containment tree. However, elements cannot be copied and pasted between separate SysML v2 projects.

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Managing elements' space='']>]]>

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Copy, Paste, Cut, and Delete commands in the element shortcut menu in the Containment tree.

#### Diagram Image Export

You can now preview and export images of SysML v2 diagrams and selected symbols using the Print, Print Preview, and Save as Image features.

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='View image export' space='']>]]>

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Print Preview, Print and Save As Image dialogs.

#### Quick Find

Now you can locate specific elements in your model by name using the Quick Find feature. Access the Quick Find dialog through the Containment tree, Main Menu, or with a keyboard shortcut to find the needed element.

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Quick find' space='']>]]>

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Using the Quick Find feature in the Containment tree toolbar.

#### Other Improvements

- The feature typing specialization is now available in the Diagram Palette, allowing you to create it as a path on a diagram, just like other specializations. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specializations' space='']>]]>
- Dragging and dropping an element from the Containment tree onto the empty diagram area now instantly creates an element symbol, skipping the drag and drop selection menu for quicker symbol display. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Displaying elements in symbolic views' space='']>]]>
- The Set Value drag and drop handler command is now available in the drag and drop selection menu, allowing you to set a feature element as a value for any other feature not only through the textual notation but also directly in diagrams. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Drag and Drop handler' space='']>]]>
- You can now create any element in the Containment tree using the Create Element dialog, which you can invoke through the Create Element command in the element shortcut menu. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Managing elements' space='']>]]>
- The **Show Specialization**property is now available in the Symbol Properties dialog, allowing you to specify whether specialization symbols should be displayed in the element name. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Modifying styles of individual symbols' space='']>]]>
- You can now create binary connectors between elements in diagrams, even when their common owner is a non-type element or not displayed on a diagram, providing greater flexibility in connector creation.
- Now you can enable the sticky header in the Textual Editor via the 'Enable the sticky header in the editor' environment option, allowing you to display the owner of the currently visible notation at the top of the editor. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Environment options' space='']>]]>

#### Model Evaluation

##### Exporting Evaluation Results to CSV

You can now export evaluation results to a CSV file for further processing with external tools or for archival purposes.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Exporting evaluation results to CSV.

##### Evaluation Analysis and Verification Cases

The SysML v2 Evaluation plugin can now evaluate Analysis and Verification cases and identify the best alternative using Trade Study analysis.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Using Trade Study analysis.

##### Metadata Evaluation Support

Now you can query model element metadata using the standard SysML v2 syntax.

**Documentation**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML v2 Plugin Documentation' space='SYSML2PAlpha15']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="trade_strudy_analysis.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Using Trade Study analysis.</h6><h3>Metadata Evaluation Support</h3><p>Now you can query model element metadata using the standard SysML v2 syntax.</p><p style="text-align: right;"><ac:link ac:anchor="top"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Back to top]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><hr /></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Documentation</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="SysML v2 Plugin Documentation" ri:space-key="SYSML2PAlpha15" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249576596 space=SYSML2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 00481: Alpha 1.6 Version News

- page_id: `249576596`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/249576596/Alpha+1.6+Version+News
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-01-08T15:53:58.843+01:00`
- ancestors: 
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

###### SysML v2 Plugin

Released on:January 6, 2025

The SysML v2 Alpha 1.6 release allows you to explore and evaluate the SysML v2 features implemented to date.

****

- 
- 
- 
- 
- 
- 
- 

- 
- 
  - 
  - 
- 

****

- 
- 
- 
- 

### SysML v2 Features

#### Custom Symbolic Diagram Types

Now you can model custom symbolic diagram types as part of your projects. By defining custom view definitions, you can model symbolic diagram types with customized diagram palettes tailored to your needs. Additionally, you can use predefined buttons or model custom ones, which can be organized into categories within the custom diagram palette. This enables greater flexibility and efficiency in modeling workflows, allowing symbolic diagrams to be precisely adapted to your specific needs while remaining part of the project for which they are created.

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Custom diagrams' space='']>]]>

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### An example of a custom symbolic diagram type *Requirements View*modeled specifically for symbolic requirements representation. 
Its custom diagram palette includes only user-specified predefined and custom-created buttons.

#### Standard Symbolic Diagram Types

In addition to the versatile General View, three new standard symbolic diagram types - Interconnection View, Action Flow View, and State Transition View - have been introduced, each tailored for specific modeling purposes. These new symbolic diagram types feature optimized palettes with button categories designed for interconnections, action flows, and state transitions, enhancing the modeling experience.

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Working with views' space='']>]]>

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The image displays General View, Interconnection View, State Transition View, and Action Flow View symbolic diagram types.

#### Create Relationship Command

You can now create relationships using the Create Relationship command, accessible from the element shortcut menu for both elements in the Containment tree and element symbols on a diagram. This provides an additional way to create relationships, making it especially convenient for working directly within the Containment tree and creating relationships between element symbols on separate diagrams.

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Managing elements' space='']>]]>

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The image displays the creation of a connection usage between two elements via the Create Relationship command in the element shortcut menu.

#### Dependency Relationship

You can now model dependencies using the dependency relationship, allowing you to represent how changes to supplier elements may affect client elements. Binary and n-ary dependencies can be created via textual notation and symbolic diagrams, offering flexibility and clarity in modeling inter-element relationships and supporting traceability across abstraction levels.

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Dependency' space='']>]]>

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The image displays a *Use*dependency where the package *Service Layer*is the client (source) and*Data Layer*is the supplier (target). The supplier end of the dependency path is indicated with an arrowhead.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The image displays an unnamed n-ary dependency where the package *Service Layer*is the client (source). The packages*Data Layer*and*External Interface Layer*are the supplier (target).The supplier end of the dependency path is indicated with an arrowhead.

#### Derivation Connection

You can now model requirements derivation using the derivation connection. This feature allows you to link higher-level requirements to more detailed, derived ones, establishing clear traceability and hierarchy in your specifications. Both binary and n-ary derivation connections can be created via textual notation and symbolic diagrams, enabling flexible and efficient requirements management.

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Deriving requirements' space='']>]]>

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']The image displays binary and n-ary derivation connectors between original and derived requirements with the appropriate labels at each connector end.

#### Easier Access to Used Project Actions

Now you can quickly access used project-related actions, such as **Open** and **Remove**, directly from the shortcut menu in the Containment tree of a project.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Opening a used project directly from the Containment tree.

#### Element Navigation Improvements

Element navigation has been improved with support for navigating among elements based on connectors between them, such as connections, successions, interfaces, etc. Using the Go To command, you can now seamlessly navigate to related elements via their connectors, further enhancing the efficiency of locating elements based on their relationships.

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Element navigation' space='']>]]>

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The Go To command's submenu displays elements related to the *vccu*element. Clicking the *'part vccu'* element under the Subsetting Feature category displays the *vccu*part element that subsets the *vccu*element for which the command was invoked.

#### Specification Panel Improvements

The Alpha 1.6 version brings several enhancements to the Specification Panel:

- New Properties Added: The Properties section now includes 'Performed Action,' 'Use Case Included,' 'Exhibited State,' and 'Result Expression' property fields for specific element types. It allows you to easily specify the performed actions, included use cases, and exhibited states for appropriate elements using the Drag and Drop handler. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification panel' space='']>]]>
- Improved Feature Value Editing: Feature value modification has been optimized for improved efficiency. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification panel' space='']>]]>
- Feature Multiplicity: You can now specify feature multiplicity. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification panel' space='']>]]>
- Inherited Features Display: The Compartments section now shows inherited features in addition to directly owned ones, with support for redefinition. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification panel' space='']>]]>

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The highlighted areas display feature value editing, feature multiplicity editing, owned and inheritedfeatures indication.

#### Textual Editor Improvements

The Textual Editor has been enhanced with the following updates.

###### Significantly Improved Textual Editor Performance

The Textual Editor’s performance has been significantly enhanced through improvements in name resolution functionality. These improvements reduce processing times and provide a smoother and more efficient modeling experience in the Textual Editor.

###### Improved Copy-Pasting of Textual Notation from the Textual Editor

Display IDs are now automatically removed when copying textual notation from the Textual Editor and pasting it elsewhere (e.g., in a notepad). This enhancement eliminates the need to switch to read-only mode or manually remove Display IDs after pasting, simplifying the process and saving time.

#### Known Issues

- An internal error may occur when the 'Collaboration Powered by 3DEXPERIENCE Platform' is enabled and a SysML v2 project is opened. This issue arises if the Magic Systems Cybersecurity Designer Plugin or Cameo Systems Cybersecurity Designer Plugin is installed, but it may also occur in other scenarios.

### SysML v2 Evaluation Features

#### Textual Representation Support for Calculations

The SysML v2 Evaluation Plugin now supports textual representation elements allowing you to evaluate scripts defined in other script languages (similar to Opaque Actions in SysML v1). Supported languages include: Javascript, Groovy, BeanShell, Python (Jython).

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Textual representation support.

#### Updated Verification Annotations

Now deeply nested failures can be highlighted in yellow, making it easier to identify issues within complex model structures. New tooltips provide clear identification of the failing components. Additionally, the new evaluation project options allow you to customize how requirement and constraint verification results are displayed in diagrams for better clarity and control. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Evaluating requirements in diagrams' space='SYSML2EPAlpha16']>]]>

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Verification annotations in a diagram.

#### Error Messages

The SysML v2 Model Evaluation panel now displays error messages to help identify and solve evaluation issues.

#### Key Fixes

- Units specified in a model no longer break the evaluation.
- Additional KerML functions have been implemented. Classification expressions, Trigonometric, Sequence, Rational, Natural, Integer, Boolean, and Real functions are now fully supported.

**Documentation**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML v2 Plugin Documentation' space='SYSML2PAlpha16']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML v2 Evaluation Plugin Documentation' space='SYSML2EPAlpha16']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE
