<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249576526 space=SYSML2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 00482: Alpha 1.7 Version News

- page_id: `249576526`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/249576526/Alpha+1.7+Version+News
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-07-17T15:07:10.783+02:00`
- ancestors: 
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

###### SysML v2 Plugin

Released on:March 17, 2025

The SysML v2 Alpha 1.7 release allows you to explore and evaluate the SysML v2 features implemented to date.

****

- 
- 
- 
- 
- 
  - 
  - 
- 
- 
- 

****

- 
- 
- 

### SysML v2 Features

##### SysML v1 to SysML v2 Migration

This release introduces the SysML v1 to SysML v2 Migration plugin, which provides model transformation capabilities, allowing you to transform SysML v1 models into SysML v2. The plugin is included in the *core.rdzip* file alongside other SysML v2 plugins. Currently, not all SysML v1 elements are supported for migration, as the plugin is a work in progress. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Performing SysML v1 to v2 model transformation' space='']>]]>

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']The image displays a SysML v1 model and its result after migration to SysML v2.

##### Tabular Views

You can now create tabular views as part of your projects. This update introduces two types of tables — Generic Table and Requirements Table — each with predefined columns that display key information about exposed model elements, such as names, owners, documentation, and more, for easy review. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Tabular views' space='']>]]>

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### An example of a Generic table.

##### View Creation Using Expressions

- Now, you can specify filter conditions for views to specify which elements should be exposed by the view, providing you more control over the views you create and the element symbols they display. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Filtering elements for views' space='']>]]>

packageMetadataDefinitions{ 
metadatadefSafety{ 
attributeisMandatory:Boolean; 
} 
}

packageVehicle{ 
privateimportMetadataDefinitions::**; 
partvehicle{ 
partinterior{ 
partalarm; 
partseatBelt[2] { @Safety{isMandatory=true;} } 
partfrontSeat[2]; 
partdriverAirBag{ @Safety{isMandatory=false;} } 
} 
} 
}

// exposes elements with *Safety*metadata attribute *isMandatory* value *true*from the exposed package *Vehicle*: 
view'Vehicle Features':'SysML Standard Diagrams'::gv{ 
exposeVehicle::**; 
filter@MetadataDefinitions::SafetyandMetadataDefinitions::Safety::isMandatory; 
}

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

- Use a rendering feature to specify how a particular view and its exposed elements should be displayed for viewing, such as tree-view or nested-view style. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Rendering views' space='']>]]>

partemployeeInformation{ 
partcandidateData; 
partemployeeData{ 
itememployeeName; 
} 
}

// an undefined view with recursive membership exposition filtering down to part usages and filtering out KerML library elements, and the *Views::asTreeDiagram* renderer, specifying the element display in tree-view style: 
view'Employee Information (tree-view)'{ 
exposeemployeeInformation::**; 
filter@SysML::PartUsage; 
filterKerML::Element::isLibraryElement==false; 
renderViews::asTreeDiagram; 
} 
// an undefined view with recursive membership exposition filtering down to part usages and filtering out KerML library elements, and the *Views::asInterconnectionDiagram* renderer, specifying the element display in nested-view style: 
view'Employee Information (nested-view)'{ 
exposeemployeeInformation::**; 
filter@SysML::PartUsage; 
filterKerML::Element::isLibraryElement==false; 
renderViews::asInterconnectionDiagram; 
}

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

- You can easily display exposed element symbols on symbolic views using the Display Exposed Elements command. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Displaying elements in symbolic views' space='']>]]>

partemployeeInformation{ 
partcandidateData; 
partemployeeData{ 
itememployeeName; 
} 
}

// a view defined by the standard predefined view definition *General View* (that contains a *'SysML Standard Diagrams'::asGraphicalDiagram*renderer) with recursive membership exposition filtering down to part usages and filtering out KerML library elements: 
view'Employee Information':'SysML Standard Diagrams'::gv{ 
exposeemployeeInformation::**; 
filter@SysML::PartUsage; 
filterKerML::Element::isLibraryElement==false; 
}

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

- For your convenience, the *CustomViewDefinitions*library package containing custom predefined view definitions is added to the modeling tool. These view definitions c ontain common element filters narrowing down exposed elements eliminating the need for you to write your own filters. To learn more, see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Symbolic views' space=''] and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Tabular views' space=''] pages. You may also review the *Views.mdzip* project sample available with the modeling tool.

partemployeeInformation{ 
partcandidateData; 
partemployeeData{ 
itememployeeName; 
} 
}

// a view defined by the custom predefined view definition *PartsNestedView* with recursive membership exposition. The *PartsNestedView* view definition already contains filters to narrow down the exposed elements to part usages and exclude KerML library elements, and the *Views::asInterconnectionDiagram* renderer: 
view'Employee Information':'CustomViewDefinitions'::PartsNestedView{ 
exposeemployeeInformation::**; 
}

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Display Features Recursively Commands

Display commands are now supplemented with Display Features Recursively, Display Parts Recursively, and Display Actions Recursively commands, allowing you to display all owned and inherited features, parts, or actionsrecursively.[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Displaying elements in symbolic views' space='']>]]>

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Enhanced Graphical Support

- Expanded Compartments Support

Compartments for parts, ports, and include use cases are now represented in the graphical notation. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Compartments' space='']>]]>

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

- Updated Interface Graphical Notation

The graphical notation for the interface usage is now updated. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Interface' space='']>]]>

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Rule-Based Symbol Styles Improvements

- Rule-based symbol styles functionality has been enhanced with additional condition types:
  - ByMetadataAnnotationCondition . Element appearance in a view (i.e. applied symbol style) can now be predicated on the type(s) of metadata annotation(s), analogous to style-for-stereotype in SysML v1.x terminology. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating style sheet components' space='']>]]>

packageNewStylesPackage{privateimportStyles::**;privateimportPolicies::*;privateimportModel::**;partdefNewStyle:>StyleSheet{partmetadataApprovalRule:>rule{part:>>condition:ByMetadataAnnotationCondition{ // the rule's condition is specified as *ByMetadataAnnotationCondition*, targeting symbols by element metadatarefitem:>>annotatingMetadataType= Safety.metadata; // the condition targets *Safety* metadata}view:ShapeStyle:>>style{attribute:>>fillColordefault"#B2BFFF";}}}}

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

- 
  - ByDiagramTypeCondition . Element appearance can now depend in the view type, allowing the same element to be displayed differently on various standard and custom view types. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating style sheet components' space='']>]]>

- Stylesheets can now include a global condition, in addition to each rule having separate conditions. This is particularly useful when combined with ByDiagramTypeCondition for creating stylesheets tailored to specific view types. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating style sheet components' space='']>]]>

// the following style sheet contains a global condition 'General View Condition' followed by an individual rule for a specific element metaclass and applicable style

packageNewStylesPackage{ 
privateimportStyles::**; 
privateimportPolicies::*; 
partdefNewStyle:>StyleSheet{ 
/* ------- global condition -------*/ 
part'General View Condition':>>styleSheetCondition:ByDiagramTypeCondition{ // global style sheet condition targeting symbols by view (diagram type) 
refitem:>>diagramType='SysML Standard Diagrams'::gv.metadata; // the condition targets *General View* views 
} 
/* -----------------------------------*/ 
partpartRule:>rule{ // an individual rule targets *PartUsage* metaclasses and applies the specified style to them. Because of the global rule, only part usages displayed on a General View will be affected by the rule 
part:>>condition:ByElementMetaclassCondition{ 
refitem:>>elementMetaclass= SysML::Systems::PartUsage.metadata; 
} 
view:ShapeStyle:>>style{ 
attribute:>>fillColordefault"#B2BFFF"; 
} 
} 
} 
}

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

- Quality-of-life improvements include multi-selection for managing styles of multiple symbols at once and background processing of style rule calculations for better performance.

##### Specification Panel Improvements

- Now, the metadata compartment organizes the features of the applied metadata, grouping them under their respective metadata labels. These displayed features include inherited features with default values and redefined features, the same way as in element symbols on views. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification panel' space='']>]]>

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

- You can now redefine inherited features from applied metadata directly through the Specification panel. This functionality simplifies the process of redefining inherited metadata features, enabling efficient redefinition without the need for the Textual Editor. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification panel' space='']>]]>
  - Clicking the Add button in the metadata compartment's bar displays a submenu with the applied metadatas and the inherited features without a set default value. Clicking a feature redefines it for the element.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

- 
  - To avoid global model changes, clicking the Navigate button next to the feature inherited from an applied metadata with a set default value opens the feature in the Specification panel in read-only mode, where you can click the Redefine button to redefine the feature, if you need to change the default value in the selected context.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

- The Specification panel now includes the Navigation tab, which allows you to easily navigate from a selected element to its related elements based on their relationships. The tab organizes related elements into categories, such as Subsetted Feature, Redefined Feature, Flow, Connection, Type, and more, clearly indicating the nature of their relationships. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification panel' space='']>]]>

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Other Improvements

- The default font in the modeling tool has been changed from Arial to Roboto, resolving previous letter spacing issues and improving text readability. Please note that on macOS, certain non-Latin characters — such as those used in Chinese or Japanese — may not render correctly.
- New SysML v2 Textual Editor environment options have been introduced:
  - *Display Metadata as Keywords* allows you to specify whether metadata should be displayed as keywords or symbols in the textual notation.
  - *Use Short Name Instead of Name for Requirement* allows you to specify whether therequirement's name or short name should be displayed in the textual notation.
  - *Max Column for Hard Wrap* allows you to s pecify the maximum number of columns for hard wrapping lines in the Textual Editor. When a line exceeds the specified limit, it is split into sections of new indented lines to fit the line length based on the maximum number of columns. When wrapping, the editor follows specific rules, such as breaking at keywords ("connect", "from", "to", "of"), binary operators ("+", "-"), commas, and long single-line bodies. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Environment options' space='']>]]>

### SysML v2 Evaluation Features

##### Manipulating Initial Runtime Values

You can now use diagrams to modify initial runtime values during evaluation. Editable values are indicated by the ":=" sign during runtime, meaning they are not bound and can be adjusted for evaluation purposes. When an initial runtime value is changed, all evaluation results are automatically recalculated and requirements are reverified.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Changing initial runtime values in a diagram.

##### MATLAB Integration Support

The SysML v2 Evaluation Plugin now supports integration with MATLAB, enabling you to use MATLAB scripts for calculations. In the previous release, we introduced textual representation elements, allowing the use of various scripting languages. With this release, once MATLAB integration is set up, you can incorporate MATLAB as an additional scripting language.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### An example of using MATLAB scripts for calculations.

##### Sequence Function Support

With this release, sequence functions are fully supported.

**Documentation**

- SysML v2 Alpha 1.7
- [ATTACHMENT filename='matlab_integration.png']
- SysML v2 Collaborative Modeling 1.7 [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML v2 Collaborative Modeling Documentation' space='SYSML2CM']

ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p><hr /></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><h1>SysML v2 Features</h1><h3>SysML v1 to SysML v2 Migration</h3><p>This release introduces the SysML v1 to SysML v2 Migration plugin, which provides model transformation capabilities, allowing you to transform SysML v1 models into SysML v2. The plugin is included in the <em>core.rdzip</em> file alongside other SysML v2 plugins. Currently, not all SysML v1 elements are supported for migration, as the plugin is a work in progress. <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Performing SysML v1 to v2 model transformation" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Learn more >>]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></p><p><br /></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="SysML v1 to SysML v2 Migration.png" /></ac:image>The image displays a SysML v1 model and its result after migration to SysML v2.</h6><h3>Tabular Views</h3><p>You can now create tabular views as part of your projects. This update introduces two types of tables — Generic Table and Requirements Table — <span>each with predefined columns that display key information about exposed model elements, such as names, owners, documentation, and more, for easy review. <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Tabular views" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Learn more >>]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="tabular_view_example.png" /></ac:image></span></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">An example of a Generic table.</span></h6></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><h3>View Creation Using elements should be exposed by the view, providing you more control over the views you create and the element symbols they display. </span><span><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Filtering elements for views" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Learn more >>]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></span></li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section {</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">expose</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">employeeInformation</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">::**;  </span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">filter</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">@ </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">SysML::PartUsage</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">;</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">filter</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">KerML::Element::isLibraryElement</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> == </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">false</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">;</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">render</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">Views::asInterconnectionDiagram</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">;</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">}</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="renderings.png" /></ac:image></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><ul><li>You can easily display exposed element symbols on symbolic views using the <span>Display Exposed </span><span>Elements command. </span><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Displaying elements in symbolic views" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Learn more >>]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_equal"><ac:layout-cell><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="4ec32ca0-3d01-4bd5-bb17-387bcb302e45"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">part</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">employeeInformation</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> {</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">part</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">candidateData</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">;</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">part</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">employeeData</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> {</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">        </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">item</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">employeeName</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">;</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">    }</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">}</span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-green-bolder,#164b35);">// a view defined by the standard predefined view definition <em>General View</em> (that contains a <em style="text-align: left;">'SysML Standard Diagrams'::asGraphicalDiagram </em>renderer) with recursive membership exposition filtering down to part usages and filtering out KerML library elements:</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">view</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">'Employee Information'</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> : </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">'SysML Standard Diagrams'</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">::gv</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> {</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">expose</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">employeeInformation</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">::**;   </span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">filter</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">@ </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">SysML::PartUsage</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">;</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">filter</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">KerML::Element::isLibraryElement</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> == </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">false</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">;</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">}</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Display_Exposed_Elements_command.png" /></ac:image></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><ul><li><span>For your convenience, the <em>CustomViewDefinitions </em>library package containing custom predefined view definitions is added to the modeling tool. These view definitions c</span>ontain common element filters<span> narrowing down exposed elements eliminating the need for you to write your own filters. To learn more, see the <span><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Symbolic views" /></ac:link> and <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Tabular views" /></ac:link> pages. You may also review the <em>Views.mdzip</em> project sample available with the modeling tool. </span></span></li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_equal"><ac:layout-cell><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="df9f8ace-8f72-401c-b05c-65321dc20e7b"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">part</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">employeeInformation</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> {</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">part</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">candidateData</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">;</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">part</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">employeeData</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> {</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">        </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">item</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">employeeName</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">;</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">    }</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">}</span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-green-bolder,#164b35);">// a view defined by the custom predefined view definition <em>PartsNestedView</em> with recursive membership exposition. The <em>PartsNestedView</em> view definition already contains filters to narrow down the exposed elements to part usages and exclude KerML library elements, and the <em>Views::asInterconnectionDiagram</em> renderer:</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">view</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">'Employee Information'</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> : </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">'CustomViewDefinitions'</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">::PartsNestedView</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> {</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">expose</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">employeeInformation</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">::**;</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">}</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="custom_predefined_view_definitions.png" /></ac:image></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><h3><span>Display Features Recursively Commands</span></h3><p style="text-align: left;"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);"><span>Display commands are now supplemented with Display Features Recursively, Display Parts Recursively, and Display Actions Recursively commands, allowing you to display <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);"> all owned and inherited features, parts, or actions </span>recursively. </span></span><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Displaying elements in symbolic views" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Learn more >>]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_equal"><ac:layout-cell><p><ac:image ac:align="right"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Display_Features_Commands.png" /></ac:image></p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Display_Features_Recursively_Commands.png" /></ac:image></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><h3>Enhanced Graphical Support</h3><ul><li><h4>Expanded Compartments Support</h4></li></ul><p><span>Compartments for parts, ports, and include use cases are now represented in the graphical notation. <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Compartments" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Learn more >>]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></span></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="compartments.png" /></ac:image></p><ul><li><h4>Updated Interface Graphical Notation</h4></li></ul><p>The graphical notation for the interface usage is now updated. <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Interface" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Learn more >>]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="interface.png" /></ac:image></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><h3>Rule-Based Symbol Styles Improvements</h3><ul><li>Rule-based symbol styles functionality has been enhanced with additional condition types:<ul><li><em>ByMetadataAnnotationCondition</em>. Element appearance in a view (i.e. applied symbol style) can now be predicated on the type(s) of metadata annotation(s), analogous to style-for-stereotype in SysML v1.x terminology. <ac:link ac:anchor="Specifying conditions for a rule"><ri:page ri:content-title="Creating style sheet components" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Learn more >>]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li></ul></li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_equal"><ac:layout-cell><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="54ec3c1e-f836-4456-9c0a-556a564fff5c"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">package</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">NewStylesPackage</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> {<br /></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">private</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">import</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">Styles</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">::**;<br /></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">private</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">import</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">Policies</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">::*;<br /></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">private</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">import</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">Model</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">::**;<br /></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">part</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">def</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">NewStyle</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> :&gt; </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">StyleSheet</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> {<br /></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">        </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">part</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">metadataApprovalRule</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> :&gt; </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">rule</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> {<br /></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">            </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">part</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">:&gt;&gt; </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">condition</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> : </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">ByMetadataAnnotationCondition</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> { <span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-green-bolder,#164b35);">// the rule's condition is specified as <em>ByMetadataAnnotationCondition</em>, targeting symbols by element metadata<br /></span></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">                </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">ref</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">item</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">:&gt;&gt; </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">annotatingMetadataType</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> = Safety.</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">metadata</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">; <span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-green-bolder,#164b35);">// the condition targets <em>Safety</em> metadata<br /></span></span><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">            }<br /></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">            </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">view</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">: </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">ShapeStyle</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> :&gt;&gt; </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">style</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> {<br /></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">                </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">attribute</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">:&gt;&gt; </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">fillColor</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">default</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-red,#ae2e24);">&quot;#B2BFFF&quot;</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">;<br /></span><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">            }<br /></span><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">        }<br /></span><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">    }<br /></span><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">}</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="ByMetadataAnnotationCondition.png" /></ac:image></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><ul><li style="list-style-type: none;"><ul><li><em>ByDiagramTypeCondition</em>. Element appearance can now depend in the view type, allowing the same element to be displayed differently on various standard and custom view types. <ac:link ac:anchor="Specifying conditions for a rule"><ri:page ri:content-title="Creating style sheet components" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Learn more >>]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li></ul></li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><ul><li>Stylesheets can now include a global condition, in addition to each rule having separate conditions. This is particularly useful when combined with <em>ByDiagramTypeCondition</em> for creating stylesheets tailored to specific view types. <ac:link ac:anchor="Creating global conditions for a style sheet"><ri:page ri:content-title="Creating style sheet components" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Learn more >>]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_equal"><ac:layout-cell><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f7ba2d36-872c-4fcb-8846-b906891989bc"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-green-bolder,#164b35);">// the following style sheet contains a global condition 'General View Condition' followed by an individual rule for a specific element metaclass and applicable style</span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">package</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">NewStylesPackage</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> {</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">private</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">import</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">Styles</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">::**;</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">private</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">import</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">Policies</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">::*;</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">part</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">def</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">NewStyle</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> :&gt; </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">StyleSheet</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> {<br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-green-bolder,#164b35);">        /* ------- <span>global condition ------- </span>*/</span></span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">        </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">part</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">'General View Condition'</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> :&gt;&gt; </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">styleSheetCondition</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> : </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">ByDiagramTypeCondition</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> { <span> <span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-green-bolder,#164b35);">// global style sheet condition targeting symbols by view (diagram type)</span></span></span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">            </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">ref</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">item</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> :&gt;&gt; </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">diagramType</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> = </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-red,#ae2e24);">'SysML Standard Diagrams'</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">::gv.</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">metadata</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">; <span> <span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-green-bolder,#164b35);">// the condition targets <em>General View</em> views</span></span></span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">        }<br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-green-bolder,#164b35);">        /* ----------------------------<span>------- </span>*/ </span></span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">        </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">part</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">partRule</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> :&gt; </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">rule</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> {  <span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-green-bolder,#164b35);">// an individual rule targets <span><em>PartUsage</em> metaclasses and applies the specified style to them. Because of the global rule, only part usages displayed on a General View will be affected by the rule</span></span></span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">            </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">part</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> :&gt;&gt; </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">condition</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> : </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">ByElementMetaclassCondition</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> {  </span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">                </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">ref</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">item</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">:&gt;&gt; </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">elementMetaclass</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> = SysML::Systems::PartUsage.</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">metadata</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">; </span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">            }</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">            </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">view</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> : </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">ShapeStyle</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> :&gt;&gt; </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">style</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> {</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">                </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">attribute</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> :&gt;&gt; </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">fillColor</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">default</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-red,#ae2e24);">&quot;#B2BFFF&quot;</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">;</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">            }</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">        }</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">    }</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">}</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="global_condition.png" /></ac:image></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><ul><li><p>Quality-of-life improvements include multi-selection for managing styles of multiple symbols at once and background processing of style rule calculations for better performance.</p></li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><h3>Specification Panel Improvements</h3><ul><li>Now, the metadata compartment organizes the features of the applied metadata, grouping them under their respective metadata labels. <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="e4804c06-dfb7-44d8-aeca-b4552c539ccf">These displayed features include inherited features with default values and redefined features, the same way as in element symbols on views. <ac:link ac:anchor="Redefining inherited metadata features via the compartment in the Specification panel"><ri:page ri:content-title="Specification panel" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Learn more >>]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link><br /></ac:inline-comment-marker></li></ul><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="grouped_applied_metadata.png" /></ac:image></p><ul><li>You can now redefine inherited features from applied metadata directly through the Specification panel. This functionality simplifies the process of redefining inherited metadata features, enabling efficient redefinition without the need for the Textual Editor. <ac:link ac:anchor="Redefining inherited metadata features via the compartment in the Specification panel"><ri:page ri:content-title="Specification panel" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Learn more >>]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link><br /><ul><li> Clicking the Add button in the metadata compartment's bar displays a submenu with the applied metadatas and the inherited features without a set default value. Clicking a feature redefines it for the element.</li></ul></li></ul><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="redefining_inherited_metadata_features_1.png" /></ac:image></p><ul><li style="list-style-type: none;"><ul><li><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="cda182c2-17fd-4e0c-8f15-5c366cbcd76e">To avoid global model changes, clicking the Navigate button next to the feature inherited from an applied metadata with a set default value opens the feature in the Specification panel in read-only mode, where you can click the Redefine button to redefine the feature, if you need to change the default value in the selected context.</ac:inline-comment-marker></li></ul></li></ul><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="redefining_inherited_metadata_features_2.png" /></ac:image></p><ul><li>The Specification panel now includes the Navigation tab, which allows you to easily navigate from a selected element to its related elements based on their relationships. The tab organizes related elements into categories, such as Subsetted Feature, Redefined Feature, Flow, Connection, Type, and more, clearly indicating the nature of their relationships. <ac:link ac:anchor="Navigation tab"><ri:page ri:content-title="Specification panel" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Learn more >>]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li></ul><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Navigation_tab.png" /></ac:image></p><h3>Other Improvements</h3><ul><li><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"><span>The default font in the modeling tool has been changed from Arial to Roboto, resolving previous letter spacing issues and improving text readability. Please note that on macOS, certain non-Latin characters — such as those used in Chinese or Japanese — may not render correctly.</span></span> </li><li>New SysML v2 Textual Editor environment options have been introduced:<br /><ul><li><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"><em>Display Metadata as Keywords</em> allows you to specify whether metadata should be <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">displayed as keywords or symbols in the textual notation.</span></span></li><li><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"><em>Use Short Name Instead of Name for Requirement</em> <span>allows you to specify whether the </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">requirement's name or short name should be displayed in the textual notation.</span></span></li><li><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"><em>Max Column for Hard Wrap</em> allows you to s</span>pecify the maximum number of columns for hard wrapping lines in the Textual Editor. When a line exceeds the specified limit, it is split into sections of new indented lines to fit the line length based on the maximum number of columns. When wrapping, the editor follows specific rules, such as breaking at keywords (&quot;connect&quot;, &quot;from&quot;, &quot;to&quot;, &quot;of&quot;), binary operators (&quot;+&quot;, &quot;-&quot;), commas, and long single-line bodies. <span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> <br /></span><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Environment options" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Learn more >>]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li></ul></li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><h1>SysML v2 Evaluation Features</h1><h3>Manipulating Initial Runtime Values</h3><p><span>You can now use diagrams to modify initial runtime values during evaluation. Editable values are indicated by the &quot;:=&quot; sign during runtime, meaning they are not bound and can be adjusted for evaluation purposes. When an initial runtime value is changed, all evaluation results are automatically recalculated and requirements are reverified.</span></p><p><br /></p><p style="text-align: center;"><span><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="changing_runtime_values.png" /></ac:image></span></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><span>Changing initial runtime values in a diagram.</span></h6><h3>MATLAB Integration Support</h3><p>The SysML v2 Evaluation Plugin now supports integration with MATLAB, enabling you to use MATLAB scripts for calculations. In the previous release, we introduced textual representation elements, allowing the use of various scripting languages. With this release, once MATLAB integration is set up, you can incorporate MATLAB as an additional scripting language.</p><p><br /></p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="matlab_integration.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">An example of using MATLAB scripts for calculations.</h6><h3>Sequence Function Support</h3><p>With this release, sequence functions are fully supported.</p><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><hr /></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Documentation</strong></p><ul><li>SysML v2 Alpha 1.7</li><li><ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="matlab_integration.png" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[SysML v2 Evaluation Alpha 1.7]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li>SysML v2 Collaborative Modeling 1.7 <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="SYSML2CM" ri:content-title="SysML v2 Collaborative Modeling Documentation" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249576494 space=SYSML2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 00483: Alpha 1.8 Version News

- page_id: `249576494`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/249576494/Alpha+1.8+Version+News
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-07-18T10:49:10.742+02:00`
- ancestors: 
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

###### SysML v2 Plugin

Released on:July 18, 2025

The SysML v2 Alpha 1.8 release allows you to explore and evaluate the SysML v2 features implemented to date.

****

- 
  - 
  - 
  - 
- 
  - 
  - 
- 
- 
- 
  - 
  - 
- 
- 
- 
- 
- 
  - 
  - 
  - 
- 

****

### SysML v2 Features

##### Model-Based Tabular Views & Columns

###### Model-Based Tabular Views

You can now model custom tabular views as part of your projects. You can either model custom tabular view definitions with custom-modeled columns from scratch or reuse predefined tabular view definitions and customize them according to your preferences. This enables greater flexibility in views modeling, allowing tabular views to be adapted to your specific needs while remaining an integral part of the model. Learn more [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Custom tabular view definitions' space='']>]]> and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating custom tabular view definitions' space='']>]]>

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Example of (1) predefined tabular view *Generic Table*, (2) customized *Generic Table* with the *Owned Element* columns removed, (3) custom *Drone Variant Table* with custom columns.

###### Table Column Modeling

Currently, two kinds of table columns are available for modeling:

- 
  - *ColumnByFeatureView*- for simple metadata feature value retrieval.
  - *ColumnByExpressionView*- for more complex metadata feature value retrieval.

The columns you create for a tabular view usage or definition must be defined by one of these column definitions, depending on the column's intended purpose. You can model custom columns for individual view usages as well as view definitions. This distinction supports both straightforward data display and more sophisticated modeling needs, enabling you to represent information in the table with the level of detail your project requires. Learn more [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Tabular view columns' space='']>]]> and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Modeling tabular view columns' space='']> ]]>

Using *ColumnByFeatureView* to model the *Owner* column:

view'Drone Requirements Table':'Requirements Table'{ 
exposeDroneStakeholderRequirements::**; 
renderrendering:>>asTable{ 
view:>>'Declared Name'; 
view:>>'Req Id'; 
view:>>Documentation;

// Using ColumnByFeatureView to model the Owner column:

viewOwner:ColumnByFeatureView:>column{ 
refitem:>>columnFeature=ownermetaFeature; 
} 
} 
}

*[IMAGE alt='' src='']*

Using *ColumnByExpressionView*to model the custom column *Weight*:

viewdef<vt>VariantTable:>'Base Table',NonStandardLibraryElementFilter{ 
filter@PartDefinitionor@PartUsage; 
renderrendering:>>asTable{ 
view:ColumnByFeatureView:>column{...}

// Using ColumnByExpressionView to model the custom column Weight: 
 
viewWeight:ColumnByExpressionView:> column { 
renderrenderingasTableCell:RealCellRendering:>asTableCell{ 
calc:>>getValue{ 
in:>>rowElement:Element; 
ifgetWeight(rowElement) @LiteralInteger? (getWeight(rowElement)asLiteralInteger).valueelse 
(getWeight(rowElement)asLiteralRational).value 
} 
calcgetWeight{ 
ine:Element; 
(((e.ownedElementasAttributeUsage)->ControlFunctions::select{ 
inee; 
(eeasElement).name=="weight" 
}asAttributeUsage).ownedRelationshipasFeatureValue).value 
} 
} 
} 
viewPrice:ColumnByExpressionView:> column {...} 
view'Net Price':ColumnByExpressionView:> column {...} 
} 
} 
view'Drone Variant Table':VariantTable{ 
filter@PartDefinitionandDefinition::isVariation; 
exposeDroneVariants::**; 
}

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Other Model-Based Tabular Views & Columns Improvements

- 
  - A new toolbar button has been added to tabular views, allowing you to open the current table view directly in the Textual Editor.
  - The isMultiline attribute has been introduced for single-valued string cells, enabling text wrapping and multiline display. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Modeling tabular view columns' space='']>]]>
  - Editing of primitive ColumnByFeatureView cell values ( e.g., String, Element, Enum, Integer, Real, Boolean ) is now supported directly within the graphical representation of the table view.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The **Open in Textual Editor**button; editing of the String value in a cell of the *Declared Name* column; text wrapping in the *Qualified Name*column cells via the *isMultiline*attribute.

##### View Creation Dialog Customization

###### Model-Based View Creation Dialog

You can now model a custom, model-based View Creation dialog that lists only the view creation commandsof your choice.

There are two options for customizing the View Creation dialog:

- 
  - Customize the predefined *SysMLViewCreationDialog*configuration in the *SysML Customization* model library. This allows you to inherit its configuration and tailor the View Creation dialog as needed. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Customizing View Creation dialog' space='']>]]>
  - Create a View Creation dialog from scratch via the conceptual *ViewCreationDialog*definition. This gives you full control of designing the dialog entirely according to your own needs — without relying on predefined settings. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating View Creation dialog' space='']>]]>

With either approach, you can define a model-based View Creation dialog that includes only the commands you need, as well as specifying how they are structured into custom command categories. This enables full customization of the dialog to match the needs of your specific project. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Custom View Creation dialog' space='']>]]>

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The image on the left shows the View Creation submenu according to the predefined *SysMLViewCreationDialog* configuration. The image on the right shows the customized View Creation submenu.

###### View Creation Dialog Management

Additionally, you can activate different View Creation dialog configurations for your project via the **Active Create View Dialog**. Use the **Manage Create View Dialog** command from the configuration element shortcut menu or the **Active View Creation Dialog** project option. While the predefined *SysMLViewCreationDialog* configuration is enabled by default for all SysML v2 projects, you can easily activate another configuration. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Activating View Creation dialog' space='']>]]>

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The **Manage Create View Dialog** command invokes the **Active Create View Dialog**, where the custom View Creation dialog's *CustomViewCreationDialog*configuration is activated for the project.

##### Cross Features Support

Cross features are now supported in the modeling tool, allowing you tospecify that a value at one end of the connection is linked to a value at the other end. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Cross features' space='']>]]>

- You can specify cross features for connection ends as owned cross features or declare them in the related types of the connection using the cross subsetting relationship. Owned cross features can be named or unnamed. Cross features in connection usages support both full and shorthand notations.

/* cross features owned by the connection definition ends */ 
partdefHub; 
partdefDevice; 
connectiondefDeviceConnection{ 
end[0..1]parthub:Hub; 
end[1..*]orderedpartdevice:Device; 
} 
partdefNetworkConfiguration{ 
partnetworkHubs:Hub[*]; 
partnetworkDevices:Device[*]; 
connectionnetworkConnections:DeviceConnection{ 
end[1]parthub::>networkHubs; 
end[0..4]orderedpartdevice::>networkDevices; 
} 
}

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

- Additionally, Move Cross Feature to End and Move Cross Feature to End Type refactoring commands are available for connection definition ends, allowing you to convert ends between the two declaration options.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### SysML v1 to SysML v2 Migration Progress

- The SysML v1 to SysML v2 Migration plugin has been enhanced, now supporting a broader range of elements. However, as the plugin is still a work in progress, not all SysML v1 elements are currently supported for migration. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Performing SysML v1 to v2 model transformation' space='']>]]>

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### A SysML v1 model and its result after migration to SysML v2.

- Additionally, if the migration includes any failures, a .*xlsx* migration status file is now generated. This file lists separate sheets for elements and properties that were not migrated. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Performing SysML v1 to v2 model transformation' space='']>]]>

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Style Sheet Improvements

###### Style Sheet Management Improvements

- You can now activate style sheets for both the entire project and for individual views. This flexibility allows you to combine multiple style sheets based on your modeling needs (for example, activating a style sheet for the entire project while activating distinct style sheets for individual views). Additionally, style sheets activated at both the project and view levels can be prioritized by setting their order of application. You can activate style sheets either via the Textual Editor or using the newly introduced **Style Sheets** button in the view toolbar. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Applying style sheets' space='']>]]>

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The *DefaultStyleSheet*is activated for the entire project via the **Style Sheets**button in the view toolbar, while the *NewStyle***style sheet, which changes the color of an interface usage, is activated for the specific view *vehicle*.

- You can also disable project-level style sheets for a specific view. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Applying style sheets' space='']>]]>

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Disabling active project style sheets for the *vehicle*view via the **Apply Style Sheet**dialog.

###### Other Style Sheet Improvements

- A new rule condition, *FreeFormCondition,*has been introduced for style sheet rules, allowing you to target element symbols by the specified expression. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating style sheet components' space='']>]]>
- Style sheet part definitions can now be displayed as symbols in views, featuring a dedicated compartment that lists their rules—each accompanied by an icon that visually reflects the defined style. [ATTACHMENT filename='defaultstylesheetpartdefinition.png']

##### Semantic and Non-Semantic Metadata Representation

As two kinds of metadata can be applied to model elements—semantic and non-semantic—representational differences have been implemented in accordance with the SysML v2 language specification. The following differences apply to element representation in the Containment tree, Specification panel, symbols on views, and any other UI instances. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Metadata' space='']>]]>

If an element has at least one semantic metadata applied to it:

- The element's metaclass keyword (e.g., part ) is not displayed.
- The applied semantic metadata name is displayed with the # sign within guillemets « ». The def keyword is appended for definition elements. [ATTACHMENT filename='semantic_metadata.png']

If an element has only non-semantic metadata applied to it:

- The applied semantic metadata name is not displayed; thus, element representation remains essentially unchanged. The element only displays metadata-related information in the metadata compartment and a metadata annotation. [ATTACHMENT filename='non-semantic_metadata.png']

##### Package Member Display

This release introduces display options for package members, providing you with greater control and visibility.

- The new Display Owned Members and Display Owned Members Recursively commands allow you to view package members in both Nested-view and Tree-view styles. [ATTACHMENT filename='Display Owned Members commands.png']
- The package element symbol now supports displaying its members directly within the members compartment, improving the visibility of the package structure. [ATTACHMENT filename='Members compartment.png']
- A new symbol property, Show Only Owned, has been added for the members compartment. It allows you to choose whether to display only the elements owned by the package or include the imported ones as well. [ATTACHMENT filename='Show only owned symbol property.png']

##### Graphical Notation Improvements for Loop and If-Else Action Usages

This release introduces several graphical notation enhancements for *for loops*, *while loops*, and *if-else actions*, improving clarity and visual consistency:

- Action conditions — such as if , while , until , and loop iterators — are now shown in dedicated compartments, rather than within the name header.
- Nested actions are now displayed in graphical compartments, instead of being listed under body, thenClause , or elseClause .
- As a result, Tree-view style display commands now display nested elements in a Nested-view style within their respective compartments.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']The *if* action with the *if condition*compartment as well as nested actions in *thenClause* and *elseClause*compartments.

##### Navigation for Elements Connected via Interfaces

The Go To commandnow supports navigation between elements connected through their owned or inherited ports via interfaces.[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Element navigation' space='']>]]>

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Specification Panel and Window Improvements

###### Context-Aware Inherited Features

To prevent unintended global model changes, clicking the Navigate button next to an inherited feature now opens that feature in the Specification panel in read-only mode. If you need to modify the default value in the current context, you can click the Redefine button to redefine the feature. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification panel' space='']>]]>

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Toggle Library Elements' Visibility in the Specification Window

You can now choose to display library elements for properties in the Specification window, providing a more comprehensive view of model elements. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='']>]]>

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Other Specification Panel and Window Improvements

- 
  - Holding down the CTRL key while dragging and dropping elements onto element-typed fields in the Specification panel (e.g., Type, Use Case Included, etc.) now allows you to add the dropped element in addition to the set element.
  - The issue where it was possible to change Declared Name, Short Name, Documentation for elements that cannot have those properties (e.g., specializations) has been fixed.

##### Other Improvements

- The View Properties dialog has been introduced, which can be invoked via the View Properties command in the view's shortcut menu. It contains the Use Gradient Fill style property allowing you to specify whether a gradient should be used for the Fill Color and Content Fill Color symbol style properties. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Modifying styles of individual views' space='']>]]>
- New symbol properties have been introduced:
  - The Show Name symbol property, allowing you to choose whether the element name should be displayed on the element symbol. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Modifying styles of individual symbols' space='']>]]>
  - The Show Short Name symbol property, allowing you to choose whether the element short name should be displayed on the element symbol. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Modifying styles of individual symbols' space='']>]]>
  - The Show Only Owned symbol property, allowing you to specify whether only the owned elements or imported ones, too, should be displayed in the symbol compartment. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Modifying styles of individual symbols' space='']>]]>
  - The Show Modifiers symbol property, allowing you to specify whether the element's modifiers (e.g., *in, ref, abstract*, etc.) should be displayed on the element symbol. The property does not control the individual and portion kind (timeslice, snapshot) modifiers. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Modifying styles of individual symbols' space='']>]]>
  - The Text Vertical Position symbol property, allowing you to specify the element name position for the symbol. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Modifying styles of individual symbols' space='']>]]>

**Documentation**

- SysML v2 Alpha 1.8
- SysML v2 Evaluation Alpha 1.8
- SysML v2 Collaborative Modeling 1.8

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><h5 style="text-align: center;">SysML v2 Plugin</h5><p style="text-align: center;"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#707070);">R</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#707070);">eleased on: </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#707070);">July 18, 2025</span></p><p><br /></p><p>The SysML v2 Alpha 1.8 release allows you to explore and evaluate the SysML v2 features implemented to date. </p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p><hr /></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_equal"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong><ac:link ac:anchor="SysML v2 Features" /></strong></p><ul><li data-uuid="586347c2-fc60-4e4a-b766-2540208923d0"><ac:link ac:anchor="Model-Based Tabular Views &amp; Columns" /><ul><li data-uuid="586347c2-fc60-4e4a-b766-2540208923d0"><ac:link ac:anchor="Model-Based Tabular Views" /></li><li data-uuid="586347c2-fc60-4e4a-b766-2540208923d0"><ac:link ac:anchor="Table Column Modeling" /></li><li data-uuid="586347c2-fc60-4e4a-b766-2540208923d0"><ac:link ac:anchor="Other Model-Based Tabular Views &amp; Columns Improvements" /></li></ul></li><li data-uuid="5ff539e7-c080-45de-b0af-5821d11d46a1"><ac:link ac:anchor="View Creation Dialog Customization" /><br /><ul><li data-uuid="b87a4c96-d081-4c0f-8abf-71eb7c4cac96"><ac:link><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Model-Based View Creation Dialog]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li data-uuid="a306b664-e2bc-4ad2-9834-6bf074aa74ac"><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"><ac:link ac:anchor="View Creation Dialog Management" /></span></li></ul></li><li data-uuid="8f57384f-e622-4dc5-8223-2d6f100bf2ae"><span><ac:link ac:anchor="Cross Features Support" /> </span></li><li data-uuid="93f19737-ffdf-4ecc-a544-9e66ca6e2633"><ac:link ac:anchor="SysML v1 to SysML v2 Migration Progress" /><span> </span></li><li data-uuid="e94362bd-0005-4a1c-848e-73c10a51006b"><ac:link ac:anchor="Style Sheet Improvements" /><ul><li data-uuid="62be47eb-062d-4f71-b5c4-a268d95aedec"><ac:link ac:anchor="Style Sheet Management Improvements" /></li><li data-uuid="c6b7c373-b3ca-48ac-957d-53bb427b189e"><ac:link ac:anchor="Other Style Sheet Improvements" /></li></ul></li><li data-uuid="8b8f9009-f9aa-47c9-b31a-c624ee6589f8"><ac:link ac:anchor="Semantic and Non-Semantic Metadata Representation" /></li><li data-uuid="3976088d-1461-4ad6-83e5-48d95f280ef1"><span><ac:link ac:anchor="Package Member Display" /></span></li><li data-uuid="78bb4673-e45f-4687-b92e-965820d691c2"><ac:link ac:anchor="Graphical Notation Improvements for Loop and If-Else Action Usages" /></li><li data-uuid="ca7b1db4-06e5-48f3-a74c-9a39b0f6b324"><ac:link ac:anchor="Navigation for Elements Connected via Interfaces" /></li><li data-uuid="dbd4f9b1-f0da-4468-a38d-486619d02569"><ac:link ac:anchor="Specification Panel and Window Improvements" /><ul><li data-uuid="8351875a-e120-40ae-b7bc-9cc0a26ba029"><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"><ac:link ac:anchor="Context-Aware Inherited Features" /></span></li><li data-uuid="fbce7570-26e0-4516-bdf8-4dc371ec37bd"><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"><ac:link ac:anchor="Toggle Library Elements' Visibility in the Specification Window" /></span></li><li data-uuid="d684d0ce-c1a5-4c8b-a706-b36f23365bf1"><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"><ac:link ac:anchor="Other Specification Panel and Window Improvements" /></span></li></ul></li><li data-uuid="733b53a8-ceb5-4128-8931-806cec7e55c3"><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"><ac:link ac:anchor="Other Improvements" /></span></li></ul></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p><strong><ac:link ac:anchor="SysML v2 Evaluation Features" /></strong></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p><hr /></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><h1>SysML v2 Features</h1><h3><span>Model-Based Tabular Views &amp; Columns </span></h3><h4 style="margin-left: 40.0px;"><span>Model-Based Tabular Views</span></h4><p style="margin-left: 40.0px;text-align: left;"><span>You can now model custom tabular views as part of your projects. You can either model custom tabular view definitions with custom-modeled columns from scratch or reuse predefined tabular view definitions and customize them according to your preferences. This enables greater flexibility in views modeling, allowing tabular views to be adapted to your specific needs while remaining an integral part of the model. Learn more <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Custom tabular view definitions" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[here >>]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> and <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Creating custom tabular view definitions" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[here >>]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></span></p><p style="margin-left: 40.0px;text-align: left;"><span><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="custom_tabular_views.png" /></ac:image></span></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Example of (1) predefined tabular view <em>Generic Table</em>, (2) customized <em>Generic Table</em> with the <em>Owned Element</em> columns removed, (3) custom <em>Drone Variant Table</em> with custom columns.</h6><h4 style="text-align: left;margin-left: 40.0px;"><span>Table Column Modeling</span></h4><p style="margin-left: 40.0px;text-align: left;"><span>Currently, two kinds of table columns are available for modeling: </span></p><ul><li style="list-style-type: none;"><ul><li data-uuid="097021d6-ace5-4c92-b1a4-ae36e95cbfa1"><span><em>ColumnByFeatureView </em>- for </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">simple metadata feature value retrieval. </span></li><li data-uuid="bff10a4d-4ec9-45b7-b86f-244a1d73a6d1"><span><em style="text-align: left;">ColumnByExpressionView </em>- for more </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">complex metadata feature value retrieval.</span></li></ul></li></ul><p style="margin-left: 40.0px;text-align: left;"><span>The columns you create for a tabular view usage or definition must be defined by one of these column definitions, depending on the column's intended purpose. You can model custom columns for individual view usages as well as view definitions. This distinction supports both straightforward data display and more sophisticated modeling needs, enabling you to represent information in the table with the level of detail your project requires. Learn more <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Tabular view columns" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[here >>]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> and <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Modeling tabular view columns" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[ here >> ]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> <br /><br /></span></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_equal"><ac:layout-cell><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-green-bolder,#164b35);">Using <em>ColumnByFeatureView</em> to model the <em>Owner</em> column:</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="894ecc7b-12bc-481d-b14e-167c227aa3bf"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">view</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">'Drone Requirements Table'</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> : </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">'Requirements Table'</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> {</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">expose</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">DroneStakeholderRequirements</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">::**;</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">render</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">rendering</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> :&gt;&gt; </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">asTable</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> {</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">        </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">view</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> :&gt;&gt; </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">'Declared Name'</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">;</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">        </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">view</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> :&gt;&gt; </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">'Req Id'</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">;</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">        </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">view</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> :&gt;&gt; </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">Documentation</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">;</span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">        </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-green-bolder,#164b35);">// Using ColumnByFeatureView to model the Owner column:</span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">        </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">view</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">Owner</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> : </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">ColumnByFeatureView</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> :&gt; </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">column</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> {</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">            </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">ref</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">item</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> :&gt;&gt; </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">columnFeature</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> = </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">owner</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">meta</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">Feature</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">;</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">        }</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">    }</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">}</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p><em><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="modeling_ColumnByFeatureView.png" /></ac:image> </em></p><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_equal"><ac:layout-cell><p>Using <em>ColumnByExpressionView </em>to model the custom column <em>Weight</em>:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="4da520ab-4128-40b2-9d31-b1a4c91a8de9"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">view</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">def</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> &lt;vt&gt; </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">VariantTable</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> :&gt; </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">'Base Table'</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">, </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">NonStandardLibraryElementFilter</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> {</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">filter</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> @</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">PartDefinition</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">or</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> @</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">PartUsage</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">;</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">render</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">rendering</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> :&gt;&gt; </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">asTable</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> {</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">        </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">view</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> : </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">ColumnByFeatureView</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> :&gt;
````
