Selecting the option ignores the specification of applied project style sheets specified in the <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><strong>Project Style Sheets</strong> dialog for the selected view, only taking into account the style sheets specified for this exact view in the <strong>Apply </strong></span><strong>Style Sheets </strong>dialog<span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">.</span></li><li data-uuid="5c6eb371-5855-4c45-9aff-1c1b717d3d86">Click the <strong>OK</strong> button. The selected style sheets are applied, and element symbols are updated in the <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">view</span>.</li></ol><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Apply Style Sheets dialog.png" /></ac:image></p><h4>Displaying style sheets applied to a specific view </h4><p>To display style sheets applied to a specific view </p><hr /><ol style="text-align: left;"><li data-uuid="c17f76ac-863f-4acc-b8ac-b3e6b179213a">Open the specific view. </li><li data-uuid="21e469ca-c40e-4943-bb5d-8aebd2f15eef">Click the<span> </span><strong>Style Sheets</strong><span> </span>button<span> in the view toolbar</span>. In the dropdown menu, click <strong>Display Applied Style Sheets </strong>command.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Display Applied Style Sheets command.png" /></ac:image><br />Part definition element symbols of the style sheets applied to the view are displayed in the view.<br /><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a7c868e7-a07c-4944-b846-32c3c12da91b"><ac:rich-text-body><p>If style sheets are applied to the entire project, but none for the specific view, the command will not display the style sheet symbols.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li></ol>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=260997144 space=SYSML2P version=14 -->
## PAGE 00488: Assert constraint

- page_id: `260997144`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/260997144/Assert+constraint
- version_number: 14
- version_date: `2026-02-17T21:32:57.808+01:00`
- ancestors: SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > Model composition > Common elements > Constraints
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

An assert constraint usage specifies that the result of a given constraint must always be true. If, at any point, the assert constraint evaluates to anything other than its asserted value, it indicates a logical inconsistency within the model.

There are two ways a constraint can be asserted:

50%

- by referencing a constraint defined elsewhere in the model.
  - The element symbol is displayed with the assert keyword (unnamed element) or the assert constraint keyword (named element). 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
  - See the procedures:
    - :
      - 
      - 
      - 
      - 
      - 
      - 

50%

- by containing an owned assert constraint usage, i.e., the asserted constraint is the assert constraint usage itself.
  - The element symbol is displayed with the assert constraint keyword. [ATTACHMENT filename='Assert constraint_2.png']
  - See the procedures:
    - :
      - 
      - 
      - 
      - 
      - 

##### Asserting a constraint by referencing a constraint defined elsewhere in the model

###### Asserting constraints via the Textual Editor

To assert constraints via the Textual Editor

1. In the body of an element for which you want to reference a constraint, declare one of the following:
  1. For an unnamed assert constraint, declare the assert keyword, then follow with the name of the constraint you want to reference.
  2. For a named assert constraint, declare the assert constraint keyword, name the new usage, and then [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Reference subsetting' space=''] the constraint you want to reference. To negate an assert constraint usage, specify the *not* keyword after the *assert*keyword, e.g., *assert not constraint*.
2. Specify the constraint (expression, attributes, etc.) in the element's body.
3. Click the Synchronize button.

constraintdefMassLimit{ 
attributemass:MassValue; 
attributemassLimit:MassValue; 
mass<=massLimit 
} 
assertconstraintmassIsPositive{ 
attributemass:MassValue; 
mass>0[kilogram] 
} 
 
partvehicle{ 
attributemassActual:MassValue; 
attributemassRequired:MassValue; 
assertmassIsPositive{ :>>mass=massRequired; }// step 1.a - unnamed usage reference subsets another constraint usageassertconstraint positiveMass::>massIsPositive{ :>>mass=massRequired; }// step 1.b - named usage reference subsets another constraint usage 
assertnotmassIsPositive{ :>>mass=massRequired; }// negated assert usage}

###### Asserting constraints via the Drag and Drop handler in a view

To assert constraints via the Drag and Drop handler in a view

- Drag and drop an assert constraint element from the Containment tree or use its symbol's [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Drag and Drop handler' space=''] and drop it on:
  - the requirement element symbol that you want to assert the constraint by referencing it using the **Create Assert Constraint****** command.
  - the assert constraint symbol that you want to assert by referencing the constraint using the **Set Asserted Constraint****** command.

###### Asserting constraints via the Specification panel

To assert constraints via the Specification panel

- Drag and drop an assert constraint element from the Containment tree or use its symbol's [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Drag and Drop handler' space=''] and drop it on the **Asserted Constraint** field in the Properties section of the Specification panel for the assert constraint element. The appropriate constraint is specified for the element.

###### Asserting constraints via the view palette

To assert constraints via the view palette

1. In the view palette, under the Requirements/Constraints group, click assert . If the needed button is not displayed, click the down-facing arrow next to the**assert constraint**button and select the needed button in the dropdown menu.
2. Do one of the following:
  1. To assert an existing constraint, in the view pane, click on the source element symbol, then on the constraint element symbol.
  2. To assert a new constraint, in the view pane, click on the source element symbol, then elsewhere in the view pane to create a constraint element symbol. The assert path is drawn between the element symbols.

###### Asserting constraints via the Create Relation command

To assert constraints via the Create Relation command

1. Right-click on the client (source) element.
2. In the shortcut menu, click Create Relation , then in the submenu, under Outgoing , click assert . The Create... dialog opens.
3. In the Create... dialog, do one of the following:
  1. In a view, click the element symbol you want to be the supplier (target). Hold Shift to select multiple elements.
  2. Click the Add [IMAGE alt='' src=''] button. The element selection dialog opens. In the element selection dialog, select the element you want to be the supplier (target) and click OK .
  3. Drag and drop the needed element you want to be the supplier (target) from the Containment tree onto the Create... dialog.
4. In the Create... dialog, click OK .

###### Asserting constraints via the Specification panel

To assert constraints via the Specification panel

- Drag and drop a constraint element from the Containment tree or use its symbol's Drag and Drop handler and drop it on the Asserted Constraint field in the Properties section of the Specification panel for the assert constraint usage. The appropriate constraint is specified for the element.

##### Asserting a constraint via the owned assert constraint usage itself as the asserted constraint

###### Asserting constraints via the Textual Editor1Asserting constraints via the Textual Editor

To assert constraints via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, place the cursor where you want to create the element and declare the keyword assert constraint for an assert constraint usage. To negate an assert constraint usage, specify the *not* keyword after the *assert*keyword, e.g., *assert not constraint*.
2. Specify the element name .
3. Specify the constraint (expression, attributes, etc.) in the element's body.
4. Click the Synchronize button.

partvehicle{ 
attribute massActual : MassValue; 
attributemassRequired:MassValue;

assertconstraintmassIsPositive{ massActual<=massRequired }// named assert constraint usageassertconstraint{ massActual<=massRequired }// unnamed assert constraint usage 
assert notconstraintmassIsPositive{ massActual<=massRequired }// named and negated assert constraint usage 
}

###### Asserting constraints via the Create Element command

To assert constraints via the Create Element command

1. In the Containment tree, right-click an element, and in the shortcut menu, click **Create Element**.
2. In the Create Element dialog, click assert constraint to create an assert constraint usage element.
3. Name the element.
4. Specify the constraint (expression, attributes, etc.).

###### Asserting constraints via the view palette1Asserting constraints via the view palette

To assert constraints via the view palette

1. In the view palette, under the Requirements/Constraints group, click the **assert constraint** button to create an assert constraint usage element.
2. Click in the view pane where you want to create the constraint.
3. Specify the constraint (expression, attributes, etc.).

###### Asserting constraints via the symbol compartment in a view

For more information, see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Compartments' space=''] page.

To assert constraints via the symbol compartment in a view

1. Do one of the following:
  1. Select an element symbol and click the Create Element button on it. In the dropdown menu, click the assert constraint element.
  2. Select an element symbol and on the right side of the assert constraints compartment, click the Create assert constraint button.
2. Specify the constraint (expression, attributes, etc.). The new element is created and displayed in the assert constraints compartment.

###### Asserting constraints via the Specification panel2Asserting constraints via the Specification panel

To assert constraints via the Specification panel

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification panel' space=''].
2. Do one of the following:
  1. In the Properties section bar, click the **Create Element** [IMAGE alt='' src=''] button. The shortcut menu appears. Click the **assert constraint**element in the shortcut menu.
  2. In the **assert constraints**compartment section's bar, click the **Create assert constraint**[IMAGE alt='' src=''] button.
3. Specify the constraint (expression, attributes, etc.). The element is created in the assert constraints compartment.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

the view pane, click on the source element symbol, then on the constraint element symbol. </span></li><li><span>To assert a new constraint, in the view pane, click on the source element symbol, then elsewhere in the view pane to create a constraint element symbol. <br /></span>The <strong>assert</strong><span> path is drawn between the element symbols.</span></li></ol></li></ol><h4>Asserting constraints via the Create Relation command</h4><p>To assert constraints via the Create Relation command</p><hr /><ol><li data-uuid="74df4133-8b32-4f85-a3ea-eaa8f920dddf">Right-click on the client element.</li></ul><h3>Asserting a constraint via the owned assert constraint usage itself as the asserted constraint</h3><h4><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249577865 space=SYSML2P version=13 -->
## PAGE 00489: Assessing layout-sensitive changes

- page_id: `249577865`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/249577865/Assessing%C2%A0layout-sensitive+changes
- version_number: 13
- version_date: `2026-05-22T10:39:28.876+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > Model composition > Views > Symbol styles > Custom style sheets
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Certain style attributes are layout-sensitive, as changing their values can affect the layout of already created views. If you have created and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Applying style sheets' space=''] a new style sheet that contains styles with attribute values different from those used previously, you need to follow the procedure below to assess and either confirm or deny the changes.

- Omitting this procedure means the modeling tool will not implement the updates of the new style and retain the previous attribute values, i.e., the symbols will be represented in the old style.
- The assessment does not affect customizations made to individual symbols via the **Symbol Style** dialog. See the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Modifying styles of individual symbols' space=''] procedure on the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Modifying styles of individual symbols' space=''] page.

Follow the procedure below if your project already contains views with displayed symbols and the style sheet contains updated values of the following attributes:

- **General style attributes**: *font*, *wrap, textVerticalPosition, textHorizontalPosition, lineStyle, orientation, isRounded, pathStyle, showPayload.*
- **Compartment style attributes**: showCompartment, showShortName,showName, showFullText, showSpecialization, showQualifiedNameForSpecialization, showModifiers, showValue, showInheritedSign, showInherited, showOnlyOwned.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']The Symbol Style dialog displays the highlighted rows of the updated attributes after activating a new style sheet within which the values of layout-sensitive attributes are updated. 
At the top of the dialog, a notification provides commands for either using the new style or retaining the old one.

##### Assessing layout-sensitive changes

To assess layout-sensitive changes

#### NOTE: Prerequisites

Prerequisites

- The style sheet is created and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Applying style sheets' space=''] .
- The project is refreshed:
  - P ress the **F5**key on the keyboard.
  - In the main toolbar, click **View**> **Refresh**.

1. In a view, right-click the affected symbol(s).
2. In the shortcut menu, click Symbol Style . The Symbol Style dialog opens.
3. Review the affected attributes in the highlighted rows. The rows display the old attribute values.
4. In the notification at the top of the dialog, click one of the following commands:
  1. Use the new style - implements the updated attribute values and displays the symbol in the new style.
  2. Retain the old style - does not implement the updated attribute values and displays the symbol in the old style. Undoing the commandTo undo the **Retain the old style** command for a single attribute:Switch to the new style by selecting the check box in the **Default**column for the specific attribute.To undo either the **Retain the old style**or**Use the new style**command for all attributes, do one of the following:Press **Ctrl+Z** on the keyboard.In the **main menu**, click **Edit**> **Undo**.
5. Click Close . Some elements can only be displayed in their appropriate compartments. If the compartment is suppressed, display it to see the elements. See the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Compartments' space=''] page.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);"><span>Certain style attributes are layout-sensitive, as changing their values can affect the layout of already created views. If you have created and <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Applying style sheets" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[applied]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> a new style sheet that contains styles with attribute values different from those used previously, you need to follow the procedure below to assess and either confirm or deny the changes. </span></span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="972dee1a-e08e-4af3-ba26-fc9cd3f2886d"><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);"><span>Omitting this procedure means the modeling tool will not implement the updates of the new style and retain the previous attribute values, i.e., the symbols will be represented in the old style. </span></span></li><li data-uuid="235d8fd5-4fb9-48c8-ad3a-cda9e5eeb83b"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);"><span><span>The assessment does not affect customizations made to individual symbols via the <strong>Symbol Style</strong> dialog. <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249577556 space=SYSML2P version=2 -->
## PAGE 00490: Assign action

- page_id: `249577556`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/249577556/Assign+action
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-09-19T15:36:48.865+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > Model composition > Behavior > Actions
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

An assignment action is used to change a *referent* feature of a *target*occurrence to a new *assigned value*. The target is specified as the result of an expression and the referent is specified as a feature chain relative to that target. The new value for the feature is determined as the result of a different expression. When the assignment action usage completes, the referent feature has the new assigned value for the target occurrence.

- The target must be an occurrence, because the values of the features of attributes do not change over time.
- If the referent feature has a multiplicity upper bound other than 1, then an assignment action can assign multiple values to it, consistent with the multiplicity of the feature. The values are all assigned atomically, at the same time.

##### Specifying assign actions

###### Specifying assign actions via the Textual Editor

To specify accept actions via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, place the cursor where you want to create the assign action and do one of the following:
  1. For a named assign action usage, declare the keyword action , specify the element name, and then the keyword assign .
  2. For an unnamed assign action usage, declare the keyword assign .
2. Follow with an expression that evaluates to the target and a feature chain identifying the referent, separated by a dot, followed by the symbol := and an expression whose result is the assigned value.
3. Click the Synchronize button.

actiondefRecordNames{ 
initemrecord:Record; 
initementries:Entry[1..*];

// The values of *entries.name* are assigned to the *names* feature 
// The target of the assign action usage is *record*, and *catalog.names* is the feature chain 
assignrecord.catalog.names:=entries.name;

// The value of the expression *entries.name + entries.surname* is assigned to the *record* feature 
// The target of the assign action usage is implicitly the action def *RecordNames* 
assignrecord:=entries.name+entries.surname; 
}

###### Specifying assign actions via the view palette

To specify assign actions via the view palette

1. In the view palette, under the Other Actions group, click the assign button to create an assign action usage element.
2. Click on an action symbol in the view pane where you want to create the assign symbol.
3. (Optional) Specify the element via the Textual Editor.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

The target is specified as the result of an expression and the referent is specified as a feature chain relative to that target. The new value for the feature is determined as the result of a different expression. When the assignment action usage completes, the referent feature has the new assigned value for the target occurrence.</p><ul><li>The target must be an occurrence, because the values of the features of attributes do not change over time.</li><li>If the referent feature has a multiplicity upper bound other than 1, then an assignment action can assign multiple values to it, consistent with the multiplicity of the feature. The values are all assigned atomically, at the same time.</li></ul><h3>Specifying assign actions</h3><h4>Specifying assign actions via the Textual Editor</h4><p>To specify accept actions via the following:</p><ol><li>For a named assign action usage, declare the keyword <em>action</em>, specify the element name, and then the keyword <em>assign</em>.</li><li>For an unnamed assign action usage, declare the keyword <em>assign</em>.</li></ol></li><li>Follow with an expression that evaluates to the target and a feature chain identifying the referent, separated by a dot, followed by the symbol := and an expression whose result is the assigned value.</li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Click the </span><strong style="text-align: left;">Synchronize<span> </span></strong><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">button.</span></li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="22fb6120-3a9e-4ef6-a65f-75a2e7b732d9"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">RecordNames</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> {</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">item</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">record</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> : </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">Record</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">;</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">in</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">item</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">entries</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> : </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">Entry</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">[</span><span style="color:var(--ds-background-accent-green-bolder,#1f845a);">1</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">..*];</span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-green-bolder,#164b35);">// The values of <em>entries.name</em> are assigned to the <em>names</em> feature</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-green-bolder,#164b35);">// The target of the assign action usage is <em>record</em>, and <em>catalog.names</em> is the feature chain</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">assign</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">record</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">.</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">catalog</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">.</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">names</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> := </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">entries</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">.</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">name</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">;</span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-green-bolder,#164b35);">// The value of the expression <em>entries.name + entries.surname</em> is assigned to the <em>record</em> feature</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-green-bolder,#164b35);">// The target of the assign action usage is implicitly the action def <em>RecordNames</em></span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">assign</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">record</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> := </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">entries</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">.</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">name</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> + </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">entries</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">.</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">surname</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">;</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">}</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h4>Specifying assign actions via the view palette</h4><p>To specify assign actions via the view palette</p><hr /><ol><li>In the view palette, under the Other Actions group, click the <strong>assign </strong>button to create an assign action usage element.</li><li>Click on an action symbol in the view pane where you want to create the assign symbol.</li><li>(Optional)<span> </span>Specify the element via the Textual Editor.</li></ol>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=261623476 space=SYSML2P version=6 -->
## PAGE 00491: Assume constraint

- page_id: `261623476`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/261623476/Assume+constraint
- version_number: 6
- version_date: `2026-04-20T13:45:29.653+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > Model composition > Requirements > Requirement constraints
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

An assume constraint states the assumed constraints imposed on the model, expressed formally or informally in a requirement or a use case.

There are two ways these constraints can be assumed:

50%

- by referencing a constraint defined elsewhere in the model.
  - The element symbol is displayed with the assume keyword (unnamed element) or the assume constraint keyword (named element). 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
  - See the procedures:
    - :
      - 
      - 
      - 

50%

- by containing an owned assume constraint usage, i.e., the assumed constraint is the assume constraint usage itself.
  - The element symbol is displayed with the assume constraint keyword. [ATTACHMENT filename='assume constraint 1.png']
  - See the procedures:
    - :
      - 
      - 
      - 

##### Assuming a constraint by referencing a constraint defined elsewhere in the model

###### Assuming constraints via the Textual Editor

To assume constraints via the Textual Editor

1. In the body of an element for which you want to reference a constraint, declare one of the following:
  1. For an unnamed assume constraint, declare the assume keyword, then follow with the name of the constraint you want to reference.
  2. For a named assume constraint, declare the assume constraint keyword, name the new usage, and then [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Reference subsetting' space=''] the constraint you want to reference.
2. Specify the constraint (expression, attributes, etc.) in the element's body.
3. Click the Synchronize button.

constraintmassIsPositive{attributemass:MassValue;mass>0[kilogram]}

requirementdefMaximumMass{ 
attributemassActual:MassValue; 
attributemassRequired:MassValue;

assumemassIsPositive{// step 1.a - unnamed usage reference subsets another constraint usage 
:>>mass=massRequired; 
}

assumeconstraintpositiveMass::>massIsPositive{// step 1.b - named usage reference subsets another constraint usage 
:>>mass=massRequired; 
} 
}

###### Assuming constraints via the Drag and Drop handler in a view

To assume constraints via the Drag and Drop handler in a view

- Drag and drop a constraint element from the Containment tree or use its symbol's [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Drag and Drop handler' space=''] and drop it on:
  - the requirement element symbol that you want to assume the constraint by referencing it using the **Create Assume Constraint** command.
  - the assume constraint symbol that you want to assume by referencing the constraint using the **Set Assumed Constraint****** command.

###### Assuming constraints via the Specification panel

To assume constraints via the Specification panel

- Drag and drop a constraint element from the Containment tree or use its symbol's [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Drag and Drop handler' space=''] and drop it on the **Assumed Constraint** field in the Properties section of the Specification panel for the assume constraint element. The appropriate constraint is specified for the element.

##### Assuming a constraint via the owned assume constraint usage itself as the assumed constraint

###### Assuming constraints via the Textual Editor1Assuming constraints via the Textual Editor

To assume constraints via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, place the cursor where you want to create the element and declare the keyword assume constraint for an assume constraint usage
2. Specify the element name.
3. Specify the constraint (expression, attributes, etc.) in the element's body.
4. Click the Synchronize button.

requirementdefMaximumMass{ 
attributemassActual:MassValue; 
attributemassRequired:MassValue;

assumeconstraintpositiveMass{// named constraint 
massRequired>0[kilogram] 
} 
}

###### Assuming constraints via the symbol compartment in a view

For more information, see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Compartments' space=''] page.

To assume constraints via the symbol compartment in a view

1. Do one of the following:
  1. Select an element symbol and click the Create Element button on it. In the dropdown menu, click the assume constraint element.
  2. Select an element symbol and on the right side of the assume constraints compartment, click the Create assume constraint button.
2. Specify the constraint (expression, attributes, etc.). The new element is created and displayed in the assume constraints compartment.

###### Assuming constraints via the Specification panel1Assuming constraints via the Specification panel

To assume constraints via the Specification panel

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification panel' space=''].
2. Do one of the following:
  1. In the Properties section bar, click the **Create Element** [IMAGE alt='' src=''] button. The shortcut menu appears. Click the **assume constraint**element in the shortcut menu.
  2. In the **assume constraints**compartment section's bar, click the **Create assume constraint**[IMAGE alt='' src=''] button.
3. Specify the constraint (expression, attributes, etc.). The element is created in the assume constraints compartment.

style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">}</span></span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h4>Assuming constraints via the symbol compartment in a view</h4><p>For more information, see the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Compartments" /></ac:link> page.</p><p>To assume constraints via the symbol compartment in a view</p><hr /><ol><li data-uuid="8c336b54-0c0b-4b47-a922-fc8fdc67666e">Do one of the following:<ol><li>Select an element symbol and click the<span> </span><strong>Create Element</strong><span> </span>button on it. In the dropdown menu, click the <strong>assume constraint </strong>element.</li><li>Select an element symbol and on the right side of the <strong>assume constraints </strong>compartment, click the<span> </span><strong>Create assume constraint </strong>button.</li></ol></li><li data-uuid="f3758544-8d65-428d-86ca-9846d19d23ce">Specify the constraint (expression, attributes, etc.).<br />The new element is created and displayed in the <strong>assume constraints </strong><span>compartment.</span></li></ol><h4 class="auto-cursor-target"><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e2087889-7d60-4776-87c1-7412e437e2b7"><ac:parameter ac:name="">Assuming constraints via the Specification panel1</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro>Assuming constraints via the Specification panel</h4><p>To assume constraints via the Specification panel</p><hr /><ol><li data-uuid="29c28227-86ac-4caa-a24a-79e2207ce305"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><ac:link ac:anchor="Opening the Specification panel"><ri:page ri:content-title="Specification panel" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Open the Specification panel]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</span></li><li data-uuid="033d2abf-131c-4ccf-a38f-8ea82c392b72"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Do one of the following:</span><ol><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">In the Properties section bar, click the <strong>Create Element</strong> <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="create_element_in_specification_pane_button.png" /></ac:image> button. The shortcut menu appears. </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Click the <strong>assume constraint </strong>element in the shortcut menu.</span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">In the <strong>assume constraints </strong>compartment section's bar, click the <strong>Create assume constraint </strong><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="create_element_in_compartment_via_specification_pane_button.png" /></ac:image> button.</span></li></ol></li><li data-uuid="0e9cfc30-4684-41e6-a879-342cb4cc6ba4">Specify the constraint (expression, attributes, etc.).<br />The element is created in the <strong>assume constraints </strong><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">compartment.</span></li></ol></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249577477 space=SYSML2P version=6 -->
## PAGE 00492: Attribute

- page_id: `249577477`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/249577477/Attribute
- version_number: 6
- version_date: `2025-09-30T14:53:30.598+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > Model composition > Structure
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

An attribute definition is a definition of attributive data that can be used to describe systems or parts. An attribute definition defines a set of data values, such as numbers, quantitative values with units, qualitative values such as text strings, or data structures of such values. An attribute usage is a usage of an attribute definition. An attribute usage is always referential, whether or not the *ref*keyword is used explicitly in its declaration. The data values of an attribute usage are constrained to be in the range specified by its definition. The values of an attribute usage are data values that remain constant over time unless the attribute is owned by an occurrence, such as an item, part, or action, in which case the value can change over the occurrence's lifetime to reflect its changing condition.

##### Creating attribute usage and definition elements

###### Creating attribute usage and definition elements via the Textual Editor

To create attribute usage and definition elements via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, place the cursor where you want to create the element and declare the keyword:
  1. *attribute def*for an attribute definition.
  2. *attribute* for an attribute usage.
2. Name the element.
3. (Optional) Specify the attribute's features in the element body.
4. Click the Synchronize button.

attributedefSensorRecord;// attribute definition

attributetemperature:'°C';// attribute usage

###### Creating attribute usage and definition elements via the Create Element command

To create attribute usage and definition elements via the Create Element command

1. In the Containment tree, right-click an element, and in the shortcut menu, click **Create Element**.
2. In the Create Element dialog, select one of the following:
  1. attribute def to create an attribute definition element.
  2. attribute to create an attribute usage element.
3. Name the element.
4. (Optional) Specify the attribute's features.

###### Creating attribute usage and definition elements via the view palette

To create attribute usage and definition elements via the view palette

1. In the view palette, under the Items/Ports/Attributes group, click one of the following:
  1. The **attribute** def button to create an attribute definition element.
  2. The **attribute** button to create an attribute usage element. If the needed button is not displayed, click the down-facing arrow next to either the **attribute**or the **attribute def**button and select the needed button in the dropdown menu.
2. Click in the view pane where you want to create the attribute symbol.
3. (Optional) Specify the attribute's features.

###### Creating attribute usage elements via the symbol compartment in a view

For more information, see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Compartments' space=''] on the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Compartments' space=''] page.

To create attribute usage elements via the symbol compartment in a view

- Do one of the following:
  - Select the element symbol for which you want to create an attribute and click the Create Element button on it. In the dropdown menu, click attribute .
  - Select the element symbol for which you want to create an attribute and on the right side of the attributes compartment, click the Create attribute button. The attribute is created and displayed in the attributes compartment.

###### Creating attribute usage elements via the Specification panel

To create attribute usage elements via the Specification panel

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification panel' space=''] of the element for which you want to create an attribute.
2. Do one of the following:
  1. In the Properties section bar, click the **Create Element** [IMAGE alt='' src=''] button. The shortcut menu appears. Click **attribute**in the shortcut menu.
  2. In the **attributes**compartment's section bar, click the **Create attribute**[IMAGE alt='' src=''] button. The attribute is created in the **attributes** compartment.

###### Auto-creating attribute definition elements via the Extract Definition commands

To auto-create attribute definition elements via the Extract Definition commands

- See the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Extract Definition and Usage' space='']page.

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249577086 space=SYSML2P version=11 -->
## PAGE 00493: Auxiliary symbolic view symbols

- page_id: `249577086`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/249577086/Auxiliary+symbolic+view+symbols
- version_number: 11
- version_date: `2026-04-24T15:07:42.770+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > Model management > Working with views > Working with symbols
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Auxiliary symbolic view symbols are available inthe **Common**group of the symbolic view palette.

##### Note

A note allows you to type any text, either plain or HTML text, and anchor it to an element symbol.

You can format the symbol styles in the[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Modifying styles of individual symbols' space=''].

###### Creating a note with an anchor

To create a note with an anchor

1. In the view palette, under the Common group, click the **note** button to create a note.
2. Click in the view pane where you want to create the symbol and type the needed text. If the needed button is not displayed, click the down-facing arrow next to either the**note******or the**note anchor******button and select the needed button in the dropdown menu.
3. To anchor the note to an element symbol:
  1. In the view palette, under the Common group, click the **note anchor** button.
  2. Click on the note symbol, then the needed element symbol. The anchor is created.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Converting between plain text and HTML text in the note

To convert between plain text and HTML text in the note

- In the view pane, click the note, then the button PLAIN to convert to plain text or HTML for HTML text.

##### Text box

A text box allows you to type any text, either plain or HTML text.

You can format the symbol styles in the[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Modifying styles of individual symbols' space=''].

###### Creating a text box

To create a text box

1. In the view palette, under the Common group, click one of the following:
  1. The **text box** button to create a text box for plain text.
  2. The **text box (html)** button to create a text box for HTML text. If the needed button is not displayed, click the down-facing arrow next to either the**text box******or the**text box (html)******button and select the needed button in the dropdown menu.
2. Click in the view pane where you want to create the symbol and type the needed text.

###### Converting between plain text and HTML text in the text box

To convert between plain text and HTML text in the text box

- In the view pane, click the text box, then the button PLAIN to convert to plain text or HTML for HTML text.

##### Separators

You can use a horizontal separator to rule off different parts of a view. The**Rotate**button allows changing the separator from horizontal to vertical position, and vice versa.

You can format the symbol styles in the[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Modifying styles of individual symbols' space='']. The symbol text can be displayed as plain text or HTML text.

###### Creating a separator

To create a separator

1. In the view palette, under the Common group, click one of the following:
  1. The **vertical separator** button.
  2. The **horizontal separator** button. If the needed button is not displayed, click the down-facing arrow next to either the **vertical separator**,**horizontal separator**, or**rectangular shape**button and select the needed button in the dropdown menu.
2. Click in the view pane where you want to create the symbol.

###### Changing the separator's position

To change the separator's position

- Select the separator on the symbolic view's pane and click the [IMAGE alt='' src=''] Rotate button at the corner of the selection.

##### Rectangular shape

You can use a rectangular shape to delineate different parts of a view.

You can format the symbol styles in the[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Modifying styles of individual symbols' space='']. The symbol text can be displayed as plain text or HTML text.

###### Creating a rectangular shape

To create a rectangular shape

1. In the view palette, under the Common group, click the rectangular shape button. If the needed button is not displayed, click the down-facing arrow next to either the **vertical separator**or**horizontal separator**button and select the needed button in the dropdown menu.
2. Click in the view pane where you want to create the symbol.

##### Image from file

The image from file command allows you to insert an image as a symbol in the view pane.

For importing images as model elements, see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Image elements' space=''] page.

###### Inserting an image

To insert an image

1. In the view palette, under the Common group, click the **image from file** button.
2. Click in the view pane. The Open dialog opens.
3. Select the needed image and click Open .

Alternatively, you can also insert an image as a symbol in the view pane:

- by dragging and dropping an image from the file system directly onto the view pane.
- by copying an image from the file system and pasting it into the view pane.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

the symbol styles in the</span><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Modifying styles of individual symbols" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[ Symbol Style dialog]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">. The symbol <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">text can be displayed as plain text or HTML text.</span></span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h4>Creating a rectangular shape</h4><p>To create a rectangular shape</p><hr /><ol><li>In the view palette, under the Common group, click the <strong>rectangular shape</strong><strong><span> </span></strong>button.<br /><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1def4b52-906d-40c9-9e8a-bd67c97b05d4"><ac:rich-text-body><p>If the needed button is not displayed, click the down-facing arrow next to either the <strong>vertical separator </strong>or<span> </span><strong>horizontal separator </strong>button and select the needed button in the dropdown menu.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li>Click in the view pane where you want to create the symbol.</li></ol><h3><span>Image from file</span></h3><p>The image from file command allows you to insert an image as a symbol in the view pane.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8ea71c0d-b4da-4083-9cac-c81657b8e250"><ac:rich-text-body><p>For importing images as model elements, see the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Image elements" /></ac:link> page.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h4>Inserting an image</h4><p>To insert an image</p><hr /><ol><li>In the view palette, under the<span> </span><strong>Common</strong><span> </span>group, click the<span> <strong>image from file </strong></span>button.</li><li>Click in the view pane. The<span> </span><strong>Open</strong><span> </span>dialog opens.</li><li>Select the needed image and click<span> </span><strong>Open</strong>. </li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="05a2e998-7bfe-4dd7-b49d-5f410bc0e67b"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Alternatively, you can also insert an image as a symbol in the view pane:</p><ul><li>by dragging and dropping an image from the file system directly onto the view pane.</li><li>by copying an image from the file system and pasting it into the view pane. </li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=263094944 space=SYSML2P version=17 -->
## PAGE 00494: Base Functions

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The table below lists the functions available in the*BaseFunctions*namespace of the Standard Libraries.

- **- dabar pvz same kaip ===, nzn ar ok**

| Function | Example | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| ==abstract function == { in ref feature x[0..1] : Anything in ref feature y[0..1] : Anything} | calc def Example { private import BaseFunctions::**; in x : KerML::Feature; in y : KerML::Feature; x.type == y.type}part vehicle : Vehicle { part mainEngine : Engine; part electricEngine : ElectricEngine;}attribute result = Example(vehicle::mainEngine.metadata, vehicle::electricEngine.metadata); Output>> result = Example(mainEngine.metadata, electricEngine.metadata)false | Returns true if x and y are equal in value.Related: |
| !=abstract function != { in ref feature x[0..1] : Anything in ref feature y[0..1] : Anything} | calc def Example { private import BaseFunctions::**; in x : KerML::Feature; in y : KerML::Feature; x.type != y.type}part vehicle : Vehicle { part mainEngine : Engine; part electricEngine : ElectricEngine;}attribute result = Example(vehicle::mainEngine.metadata, vehicle::electricEngine.metadata); Output>> result = Example(mainEngine.metadata, electricEngine.metadata) true | Returns true if x and y are not equal in value. |
| ===abstract function === { in ref feature x[0..1] : Anything in ref feature y[0..1] : Anything} | calc def Example { private import BaseFunctions::**; in x : KerML::Feature; in y : KerML::Feature; x.type === y.type}part vehicle : Vehicle { part mainEngine : Engine; part electricEngine : ElectricEngine;}attribute result = Example(vehicle::mainEngine.metadata, vehicle::electricEngine.metadata); Output>> result = Example(mainEngine.metadata, electricEngine.metadata) false | Returns true if x and y are identical objects (the same instance).Related:The operators === and !== apply specifically to values that are occurrences, testing whether two occurrences are portions (in space and/or time) of the same life occurrence. For data values, === and !== are the same as == and !=. |
| !==abstract function !== { in ref feature x[0..1] : Anything in ref feature y[0..1] : Anything} | calc def Example { private import BaseFunctions::**; in x : KerML::Feature; in y : KerML::Feature; x.type !== y.type}part vehicle : Vehicle { part mainEngine : Engine; part electricEngine : ElectricEngine;}attribute result = Example(vehicle::mainEngine.metadata, vehicle::electricEngine.metadata); Output>> result = Example(mainEngine.metadata, electricEngine.metadata) true | Returns true if x and y are not identical objects (not the same instance).The operators === and !== apply specifically to values that are occurrences, testing whether two occurrences are portions (in space and/or time) of the same life occurrence. For data values, === and !== are the same as == and !=. |
| ToStringabstract function ToString { in ref feature x[0..1] : Anything} | calc def Example { private import BaseFunctions::**; in x; ToString(x)}attribute result = Example(5);Output>> result = Example(5) 5 | Returns the string representation of x.Related: |
| [abstract function [ { in ref feature x[0..*] : Anything in ref feature y[0..*] : Anything} | Output |  |
| #abstract function # { in ref feature seq[0..*] : Anything in ref feature index[1..*] : Positive} | calc def Example { private import BaseFunctions::**; in x; in y; x#(y)}attribute a = (10, 20, 30);attribute b = 2;attribute result = Example(a, b);Output>> result = Example(a, b) 20 | Returns the element at the specified index position in the sequence. Indexing starts at 1.Related: |
| ,abstract function , { in ref feature seq1[0..*] : Anything in ref feature seq2[0..*] : Anything} | calc def Example { private import SequenceFunctions::**; in x; in y; union(x, y)}attribute a = (2, 3);attribute b = (4, 3);attribute result = Example(a, b);Output>> result = Example(a, b) 2 3 4 3 | Enables concatenation of a sequence of values from the inputs. |
| allabstract function all { } | Output | Returns a collection containing all elements of x. Used to ensure full inclusion when chaining functions. |
| istypeabstract function istype { in ref feature seq[0..*] : Anything in ref feature type : Anything} | calc def Example { private import BaseFunctions::**; in x; (x as KerML::Feature).ownedFeature istype SysML::ItemUsage}part vehicle { part mainEngine; part electricEngine;} attribute result = Example(vehicle.metadata);Output>> result = Example(vehicle.metadata) true | Returns true if all values of x are instances of type t, or of a subtype of t. |
| hastypeabstract function hastype { in ref feature seq[0..*] : Anything in ref feature type : Anything} | calc def Example { private import BaseFunctions::**; in x; (x as KerML::Feature).ownedFeature hastype SysML::ItemUsage}part vehicle { part mainEngine; part electricEngine;} attribute result = Example(vehicle.metadata);Output>> result = Example(vehicle.metadata) false | Returns true if all values of x are instances of type t. |
| @abstract function @ { in ref feature seq[0..*] : Anything in ref feature type : Anything} | calc def Example { private import BaseFunctions::**; in x; (x as KerML::Feature).ownedFeature @ SysML::ItemUsage}part vehicle { part mainEngine; part electricEngine; attribute mass;} attribute result = Example(vehicle.metadata); Output>> result = Example(vehicle.metadata) true | Returns true if at least one value of x is an instance of type t, or of a subtype of t. Similar to istype. |
| @@abstract function @@ { in ref feature seq[0..*] : Metaobject in ref feature type : Metaobject} | calc def Example { private import BaseFunctions::**; vehicle @@ SysML::ItemUsage // OR vehicle.metadata @ SysML::ItemUsage}part vehicle;attribute result = Example(vehicle); Output>> result = Example(vehicle) true | Tests whether (and returns true if) any metadata associated with an element are classified by the given metaclass. Shorthand for the operator @ and a metadata access expression (.metadata). |
| asabstract function as { in ref feature seq[0..*] : Anything} | calc def Example { private import BaseFunctions::*; in element : SysML::Package; (element as SysML::Package).ownedMember as SysML::PartUsage}package UserModel { part a; part b; item c;}attribute result = Example(UserModel.metadata);Output>> result = Example(UserModel.metadata) MetadataFeature PartUsage a MetadataFeature PartUsage b | Casts x to the specified classifier, i.e., selects elements of the given input that are instances (or subtypes (performs istype)) of the specified classifier. |
| metaabstract function meta { in ref feature seq[0..*] : Metaobject} | calc def Example { private import BaseFunctions::**; (vehicle meta SysML::PartUsage) istype SysML::ItemUsage // OR (vehicle.metadata as SysML::PartUsage) istype SysML::ItemUsage}part vehicle;attribute result = Example(vehicle);Output>> result = Example(vehicle)true | Filters the metadata associated with an element and evaluates to those that are classified by the given metaclass. Shorthand for the operator as and a metadata access expression (.metadata). |

data-uuid="3abb4a74-52fe-44fb-9608-a1d71e82f3fe">Returns true if x and y are identical objects (the same instance).</li><li data-uuid="63319f5d-49f0-4275-b81b-969ceb7f4aa5">Related:<ul><li operators === and !== apply specifically to values that are occurrences, testing whether two occurrences are portions (in space and/or time) of the same life occurrence. For data values, === and !== are the same as == and !=.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></div></td></tr><tr><td><div class="content-wrapper"><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">result</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> = </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">Example</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">(</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">vehicle</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">);</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1a7f6eb3-57c7-459c-a5e7-d400f8f18c86"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Output</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>&gt;&gt; result = Example(vehicle)<br />true</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></div></td><td><ul><li data-uuid="276006f4-f538-4a3d-b4bb-ab7802b3253d">Filters the metadata associated with an element and evaluates to those that are classified by the given metaclass. Shorthand for the operator <em>as</em> and a metadata access expression (.metadata). </li></ul></td></tr></tbody></table>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249576953 space=SYSML2P version=2 -->
## PAGE 00495: Basic textual notation tasks

- page_id: `249576953`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/249576953/Basic+textual+notation+tasks
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-09-19T15:15:19.065+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > Model management > Working with Textual Editor > Managing textual notation
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Review the procedures on this page for basic textual notation tasks.

###### Moving selected textual notation

You can move selected portions of textual notation with a simple drag and drop. This functionality is more efficient than copying, deleting, and pasting the data you want to move.

To move selected textual notation

1. Select the portion of the textual notation you want to move to a different part of the notation. You can do that with:
  1. Left mouse button - select consecutive notation.
  2. Mouse's scroll wheel - select notation in a rectangular shape (see the procedure).
2. Click the left mouse button on the selected portion.
3. Drag the selection to the intended location.

###### Common basic textual notation procedures

- For procedures on creating, deleting, copying, pasting, or cutting the textual notation using the Textual Editor, see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Managing elements' space=''] .

###### Copy/paste notation using rectangular selection

You can select the textual notation in a rectangular shape by using your mouse's scroll wheel. This functionality is especially useful when you need to copy large portions of aligned notation or copy/paste repetitive data.

To copy/paste notation using rectangular selection

1. 
  1. Click and hold your mouse's scroll wheel, then select the portion of the notation you want to copy.
  2. Click Ctrl+C shortcut keys to copy the selection. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
  3. Click and hold your mouse's scroll wheel, then select the portion of the notation you want to replace with the notation copied in Step 1.
  4. Click Ctrl+V shortcut keys to paste the copied notation. The selected notation is replaced with the copied one. [ATTACHMENT filename='rectangular_selection_2.png']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Review the procedures on this page for basic textual notation tasks.</p><h4>Moving selected textual notation</h4><p>You can move selected portions of textual notation with a simple drag and drop. This functionality is more efficient than copying, deleting, and pasting the data you want to move. </p><p>To move selected textual notation</p><hr /><ol><li>Select the portion of the textual notation you want to move to a different part of the notation. You can do that with:<ol><li>Left mouse button - select consecutive notation.</li><li>Mouse's scroll wheel - select notation in a rectangular shape (see the <ac:link ac:anchor="Copy/paste notation using rectangular selection" /> procedure).</li></ol></li><li>Click the left mouse button on the selected portion.</li><li>Drag the selection to the intended location.</li></ol><h4>Common basic textual notation procedures</h4><ul><li>For procedures on creating, deleting, copying, pasting, or cutting the textual notation using the Textual Editor, see the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Managing elements" /></ac:link>.</li></ul><h4 style="margin-left: 40.0px;">Copy/paste notation using rectangular selection</h4><p style="margin-left: 40.0px;">You can select the textual notation in a rectangular shape by using your mouse's scroll wheel. This functionality is especially useful when you need to copy large portions of aligned notation or copy/paste repetitive data. </p><p style="margin-left: 40.0px;">To copy/paste notation using rectangular selection</p><hr style="margin-left: 40.0px;" /><ol><li style="list-style-type: none;"><ol><li>Click and hold your mouse's scroll wheel, then select the portion of the notation you want to copy.</li><li>Click Ctrl+C shortcut keys to copy the selection.<br /><span style="color:var(--ds-background-accent-red-bolder,#c9372c);"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="rectangular_selection_1.png" /></ac:image></span></li><li>Click and hold your mouse's scroll wheel, then select the portion of the notation you want to replace with the notation copied in Step 1.</li><li>Click Ctrl+V shortcut keys to paste the copied notation.<br />The selected notation is replaced with the copied one.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="rectangular_selection_2.png" /></ac:image></li></ol></li></ol>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249577294 space=SYSML2P version=7 -->
## PAGE 00497: Binding

- page_id: `249577294`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/249577294/Binding
- version_number: 7
- version_date: `2025-10-07T13:07:13.349+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > Model composition > Relations > Connectors
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A binding is a binary connection that:

- asserts that the connected features have equal values in the same context. See the procedures on this page.
- binds a referential feature in one context to a composite feature in another context to assert they are the same thing. [ATTACHMENT filename='display_binding.png']

###### The image displays the *fuelFlowBinding* binding connector where *fuelTank.fuelFlowOut* is bound to *engine.fuelFlowIn.*Additionally, the connector is also [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Displaying elements in symbolic views' space=''].

##### Binding elements

###### Binding elements via the Textual Editor

To bind elements via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, place the cursor where you want to create the element and declare the *binding*keyword.
2. Specify the element name (optional), [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specializations' space=''] (optional), the keyword bind, then the name of the first element you want to bind, followed by the textual symbol =, then the name of the second element you want to bind. If the binding has no name and no specialization, the keyword *bind*can be omitted.
3. Click the Synchronize button. partdefVehicle{ 
partfuelTank:FuelTank{ 
outfuelFlowOut:Fuel; 
} 
partengine:Engine{ 
infuelFlowIn:Fuel; 
} 
bindingfuelFlowBindingbindfuelTank.fuelFlowOut=engine.fuelFlowIn;// named binding 
bindfuelTank.fuelFlowOut=engine.fuelFlowIn;// unnamed binding 
}

###### Binding elements via the smart manipulator in a view

To bind elements via the smart manipulator in a view

1. In a view, select an element symbol, and in its smart manipulator, click the bind command.
2. Click on the element you want to bind. A binding path is created between the elements.

###### Binding elements via the view palette

To bind elements via the view palette

1. In the view palette, under the Connectors group, click the **bind** button to create a binding usage.
2. In a view, click on the first of the two element symbols you want to bind, then on the second. A bind symbol is created between the elements.

###### Binding elements via the Create Relation command

To bind elements via the Create Relation command

1. Right-click one of the elements you want to bind.
2. In the shortcut menu, click Create Relation , then in the submenu, under Outgoing , click binding . The Create... dialog opens.
3. In the Create... dialog, do one of the following:
  1. In a view, click the element symbol you want to bind. Hold Shift to select multiple elements.
  2. Click the Add [IMAGE alt='' src=''] button. The element selection dialog opens. In the element selection dialog, select the element you want to bind and click OK .
  3. Drag and drop the needed element you want to bind from the Containment tree onto the Create... dialog.
4. In the Create... dialog, click OK .

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>A binding is a binary connection that:</p><ul><li>asserts that the connected features have equal values in the same context. See the procedures on this page.</li><li>binds a referential feature in one context to a composite feature in another context to assert they are the same thing.<span <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=263094947 space=SYSML2P version=12 -->
## PAGE 00498: Boolean Functions

- page_id: `263094947`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/263094947/Boolean+Functions
- version_number: 12
- version_date: `2025-11-13T09:46:37.412+01:00`
- ancestors: SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > Model analysis > Functions
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The table below lists the functions available in the *BooleanFunctions* namespace of the Standard Libraries.

- 
- 
- 
- 
- 
- 
- 

| Function | Example | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| notfunction not { in ref feature x[1] : Boolean} | calc def Example { private import BooleanFunctions::**; in x; not(x)}attribute switch = false;attribute result = Example(switch);Output>> result = Example(switch) true | Returns true if x is false, and false if x is true (inverts a Boolean value).Related: |
| xorfunction xor { in ref feature x[1] : Boolean in ref feature y[1] : Boolean} | calc def Example { private import BooleanFunctions::**; in x; in y; x xor y }attribute switchOne = true;attribute switchTwo = false;attribute result = Example(switchOne, switchTwo);Output>> result = Example(switchOne, switchTwo) true | Returns true if either x or y is true, but not both.Related: |
| Logical ORfunction \| { in ref feature x[1] : Boolean in ref feature y[1] : Boolean} | calc def Example { private import BooleanFunctions::**; in x; in y; x \| y }attribute switchOne = true;attribute switchTwo = false;attribute result = Example(switchOne, switchTwo);Output>> result = Example(switchOne, switchTwo) true | Returns true if either x or y, or both, are true.Related: |
| &function & { in ref feature x[1] : Boolean in ref feature y[1] : Boolean} | calc def Example { private import BooleanFunctions::**; in x; in y; x & y }attribute switchOne = true;attribute switchTwo = false;attribute result = Example(switchOne, switchTwo);Output>> result = Example(switchOne, switchTwo) false | Returns true if both x and y are true. Related: |
| ==function == { in ref feature x[0..1] : Boolean in ref feature y[0..1] : Boolean} | calc def Example { private import BooleanFunctions::**; in x; in y; x == y }attribute switchOne = true;attribute switchTwo = false;attribute result = Example(switchOne,switchTwo);Output>> result = Example(switchOne, switchTwo) false | Returns true if x and y are equal in value.Related: |
| ToStringfunction ToString { in ref feature x[1] : Boolean} | calc def Example { private import BooleanFunctions::**; in x; ToString(x)}attribute switch = false;attribute result = Example(switch);Output>> result = Example(switch) false | Returns the string representation of the Boolean value x ("true" or "false").Related: |
| ToBooleanfunction ToBoolean { in ref feature x[1] : String} | calc def Example { private import BooleanFunctions::**; in x; ToBoolean(x)}attribute data = "true";attribute result = Example(data);Output>> result = Example(data) true | Returns the Boolean value represented by the string x ("true" → true, "false" → false). |

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249577892 space=SYSML2P version=7 -->
## PAGE 00499: Calculation

- page_id: `249577892`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/249577892/Calculation
- version_number: 7
- version_date: `2025-11-18T10:56:33.398+01:00`
- ancestors: SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > Model composition > Analysis & Verification
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A calculation is a kind of action that has a distinguished parameter with direction *out*called the result parameter. A calculation specifies a reusable computation that returns a result in the result parameter. In addition to its parameters, a calculation may have features that are calculation or action usages that carry out steps in the computation of the result of the calculation. The calculation may also have other features that are used to record intermediate results in the computation. A calculation always has a result parameter, inherited if not owned, which can be declared as an out parameter using the keyword *return* instead of *out*.

For information on how to evaluate calculations, see [Invoking calculations](https://docs.nomagic.com/SYSML2EP/?contextKey=invoking-calculations&version=latest) in SysML v2 Evaluation Plugin documentation.

##### Creating calculation usage and definition elements

###### Creating calculation usage and definition elements via the Textual Editor

To create calculation usage and definition elements via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, place the cursor where you want to create the element and declare the keyword:
  1. *calc def*for a calculation definition.
  2. *calc* for a calculation usage.
2. Specify the element name.
3. Specify the calculation's features in the element body.
4. Click the Synchronize button. calcdefDimensions{ 
inlength:Real; 
inwidth:Real; 
return:Real; 
} 
calcarea:Dimensions{ 
// Parameters redefine parameters of Dimensions 
inlength; 
inwidth; 
returnresult; 
length*width 
//Result expression is implicitly bound to the return parameter 
}

###### Creating calculation usage and definition elements via the Create Element command

To create calculation usage and definition elements via the Create Element command

1. In the Containment tree, right-click an element, and in the shortcut menu, click **Create Element**.
2. In the Create Element dialog, select one of the following:
  1. calc def to create a calculation definition element.
  2. calc to create a calculation usage element.
3. Name the element.
4. Specify the calculation's features.

###### Auto-creating calculation definition elements via the Extract Definition commands

To auto-create calculation definition elements via the Extract Definition commands

- See the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Extract Definition and Usage' space='']page.

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249577880 space=SYSML2P version=4 -->
## PAGE 00500: Case

- page_id: `249577880`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/249577880/Case
- version_number: 4
- version_date: `2025-09-30T14:55:12.310+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > Model composition > Analysis & Verification
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A case is a kind of calculation that produces a result intended to achieve a specific objective regarding a given subject. A case is a general concept that may be used in its own right, but also provides the basis for more specific kinds of cases, including analysis cases, verification cases, and use cases.

##### Creating case usage and definition elements

###### Creating case usage and definition elements via the Textual Editor

To create case usage and definition elements via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, place the cursor where you want to create the element and declare the keyword:
  1. *case def*for a case definition.
  2. *case* for a case usage.
2. Specify the element name.
3. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Subjects, actors, and objectives for cases' space=''] in the element body.
4. Click the Synchronize button. casedefFaultRecovery{// case definition 
subjectsystem:AutomationSystem; 
actorengineer:Person; 
objective{ 
doc 
/* The engineer determines the cause of the systemfault and resolves it returning the system tonominal operation.*/ 
} 
}casefaultRecovery:FaultRecovery;// case usage

###### Creating case usage and definition elements via the Create Element command

To create case usage and definition elements via the Create Element command

1. In the Containment tree, right-click an element, and in the shortcut menu, click **Create Element**.
2. In the Create Element dialog, select one of the following:
  1. case def to create a case definition element.
  2. case to create a case usage element.
3. Name the element.
4. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Subjects, actors, and objectives for cases' space=''] .

###### Auto-creating case definition elements via the Extract Definition commands

To auto-create case definition elements via the Extract Definition commands

- See the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Extract Definition and Usage' space='']page.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

Element</strong>.</p></li><li>In the <strong>Create Element</strong> dialog, select one of the following:<ol><li><strong>case def</strong> to create a case definition element.</li><li><strong>case </strong>to create a case usage element.</li></ol></li><li>Name the element.</li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Subjects, actors, and objectives for cases" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Specify the case's features]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</li></ol><h4>Auto-creating case definition elements via the Extract Definition commands</h4><p><span>To auto-create case definition elements via the Extract Definition commands</span></p><hr /><ul><li><span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">See the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Extract Definition and Usage" /></ac:link></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">page.</span></span></li></ul>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=286557494 space=SYSML2P version=3 -->
## PAGE 00501: CATIA Magic/Cameo SysML v2 Community Edition

- page_id: `286557494`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/286557494/CATIA+Magic+Cameo+SysML+v2+Community+Edition
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2026-03-25T07:26:42.000+01:00`
- ancestors: SysML v2 Plugin Documentation
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

With the Object Management Group (OMG) releasing **SysML v2**, the community is eager to explore and learn this new language. To support this journey, our teams are introducing **CATIA Magic/Cameo SysML v2 Community Edition**– a game-changer for anyone looking to master**SysML v2**!

- 100% Standard Conformance
  - Full adherence to the OMG SysML v2 specification metamodel.
- Textual and Graphical Syntax in Perfect Sync
  - Instant two-way synchronization between textual and graphical representations.
- Designed for Learning and Training
  - Model up to 500 major elements - ideal for practice and language exploration.
- Free of Charge

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Downloading the CATIA Magic/Cameo SysML v2 Community Edition

To download the CATIA Magic/Cameo SysML v2 Community Edition

1. Go to https://software.3ds.com/
2. Create an account (if you don't have one yet) and sign in.
3. Click Free Download > CATIA Magic/No Magic .
4. Find CATIA Magic SysML v2 Community Edition in the list.
5. Click 1/5 for Windows, 2/5 for Mac, 3/5 for Linux, 4/5 for Windows no install, or 5/5 for Mac aarch. [ATTACHMENT filename='download_community_ed.png']
6. Install the product you just downloaded (Magic Systems of Systems Architect, or MSOSA for short).
7. Launch the Magic Systems of Systems Architect.
8. In the License Manager, select Use in Community Mode . [ATTACHMENT filename='License Manager.png']
9. Read the license agreement and click I agree .
10. Once the Welcome screen loads, you will see a prompt indicating that the SysML v2 plugin must be installed.
11. Click the Install button in the prompt. The Resource Manager opens with the SysML v2 plugin already selected. [ATTACHMENT filename='Resource Manager.png']
12. In the Resource Manager , click the Download/Install button to install the SysML v2 plugin.
13. Restart the tool. You are now running the modeling tool in SysML v2 Community Edition Mode.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

The <strong>Resource Manager</strong> opens with the SysML v2 plugin already selected.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Resource Manager.png" /></ac:image></li><li data-uuid="79e49ef7-4fc2-4ea3-873c-759bfa5b5675">In the <strong>Resource Manager</strong>, click the <strong>Download/Install </strong>button to install the SysML v2 plugin.</li><li data-uuid="1de2c8c3-1810-4e8d-a533-7d7693400e0c">Restart the tool. - space_key: `SYSML2P`

- version_number: 14

- ancestors: SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > Model analysis > Functions
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The table below lists the functions available in the*CollectionFunctions*namespace of the Standard Libraries.

- 
- → **ne taip kazkaip darau**

| Function | Example | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| ==function == { in ref feature col1[0..1] : Collection in ref feature col2[0..1] : Collection} | calc def Example { private import CollectionFunctions::**; in x; in y; x == y}attribute a = (10, 20, 30);attribute b = (20, 10, 30);attribute result = Example(a, b);Output>> result = Example(a, b) true | Returns true if collections x and y contain the same elements.Related: |
| sizefunction size { in ref feature col[1] : Collection} | calc def Example { private import CollectionFunctions::**; in x; size(x)}attribute a = (10, 20, 30);attribute result = Example(a);Output>> result = Example(a) 3^ EXPECTED OUTPUT - 3Actual output: 0 | Returns the number of elements in the collection x.Related: |
| isEmptyfunction isEmpty { in ref feature col[1] : Collection} | calc def Example { private import CollectionFunctions::**; in x; isEmpty(x)}attribute a;attribute b;attribute c = (a, b);attribute result = Example(c);Output>> result = Example(c) false^ EXPECTED OUTPUT falseActual output: true | Returns true if the collection x contains no elements.Related: |
| notEmptyfunction notEmpty { in ref feature col[1] : Collection} | calc def Example { private import CollectionFunctions::**; in x; notEmpty(x)}attribute a;attribute b;attribute c = (a, b);attribute result = Example(c);Output>> result = Example(c) ^ EXPECTED OUTPUT: trueActual output: false | Returns true if the collection x contains one or more elements.Related: |
| containsfunction contains { in ref feature col[1] : Collection in ref feature values[*] : Anything} | calc def Example { private import CollectionFunctions::**; in x; in y; contains(x, y)}attribute a = (b, c);attribute b;attribute c;attribute result = Example(a, b);Output>> result = Example(a, b) true^ EXPECTED OUTPUT - true Actual output: false | Returns true if the collection x contains the element y. |
| containsAllfunction containsAll { in ref feature col[1] : Collection in ref feature col2[2] : Collection} | calc def Example { private import CollectionFunctions::**; in x; in y; containsAll(x, y)}attribute a = (10, 20, 30);attribute b = (10, 20);attribute result = Example(a, b);Output>> result = Example(a, b) false^ EXPECTED OUTPUT false. Actual output:Could not evaluate value of 'elements' Feature. Cause: No target for 'elements' Feature. | Returns true if the collection x contains all elements of the collection y. |
| headfunction head { in ref feature col[1] : Collection} | calc def Example { private import CollectionFunctions::**; in x; head(x)}attribute a = (10, 20, 30);attribute result = Example(a);Output>> result = Example(a) 10^ EXPECTED OUTPUT 10. Actual output:Could not evaluate value of 'elements' Feature. Cause: No target for 'elements' Feature. | Returns the first element of the collection x.Related: |
| tailfunction tail { in ref feature col[1] : Collection} | calc def Example { private import CollectionFunctions::**; in x; tail(x)}attribute a = (10, 20, 30);attribute result = Example(a);Output>> result = Example(a) 20 30^ EXPECTED OUTPUT 20 30. Actual output:Could not evaluate value of 'elements' Feature. Cause: No target for 'elements' Feature. | Returns a collection containing all elements of x except the first.Related: |
| lastfunction last { in ref feature col[1] : Collection} | calc def Example { private import CollectionFunctions::**; in x; last(x)}attribute a = (10, 20, 30);attribute result = Example(a);Output>> result = Example(a)30^ EXPECTED OUTPUT 30. Actual output:Could not evaluate value of 'elements' Feature. Cause: No target for 'elements' Feature. | Returns the last element of the collection x.Related: |
| element at index positionfunction # { in ref feature col[1] : Collection in ref feature index[1] : Positive} | calc def Example { private import CollectionFunctions::**; in x; in y; (x) # (y)}attribute a = (10, 20, 30);attribute b = (2);attribute result = Example(a, b);Output>> result = Example(a, b) 20 | Returns the element at the specified index position in the collection. Indexing starts at 1.Related: |
| element at index position2function array# { in ref feature arr[1] : Array in ref feature indexes[n] : Positive} | calc def Example { private import CollectionFunctions::**; in x; in y; x#(y)}attribute a = (10, 20, 30);attribute b = (2);attribute result = Example(a, b);Output>> result = Example(a, b) 20 | Returns the element at the specified index position in the collection. Indexing starts at 1. Functionally equivalent to (x) # (y). |
